That downgrades the finding from the implied P0/P1 data-loss to P2: a real UX/safety inconsistency worth fixing (add confirm=true or force plan-first to apply), with a recoverable blast radius.

title: set_env whole-list replace has no confirm gate — partial call wipes all env vars
summary: set_env performs a whole-list replace (every omitted key is deleted from the service) but, unlike the far less destructive bootstrap_project/add_service/update_project tools on the same surface, requires no confirm flag — one incremental-intent call from an agent silently destroys the service's entire plain env configuration.
evidence: env.go:59-66 posts {envVars: args.EnvVars} to POST .../env with no gate beyond ReadOnly; the server's SetEnv (apiv1.SetEnvRequest, handlers/projects.go:255) replaces the full set. Meanwhile projects.go:158/313 enforce confirm=true for creating a project or adding a service — operations with no data-loss potential at all.
failure_scenario: User asks the agent to 'add FOO=bar to the api service'. The agent calls set_env with envVars:[{FOO,bar}] only (the single-var mental model), the server replaces the whole list, every other plain env var is removed, and the next env refresh rolls the pods with a broken configuration — no confirmation, no diff shown.
fix_sketch: Add confirm=true (or a merge-mode default that fetches current vars via GET .../env and merges, with replace available behind an explicit flag), matching the gating convention the rest of the tool surface already uses.
verify_reason: Traced end-to-end: mcp/internal/tools/env.go:59-72 posts {envVars} to POST .../env with no confirm flag (only the centralized read-only check in kusoclient.PostJSON, client.go:60), while sibling tools bootstrap_project/update_project/add_service/manage_addon in mcp/internal/tools/projects.go (lines 158/225/313/377) all hard-require confirm=true. Server-side, services_ops.go:1387 (`svc.Spec.EnvVars = convertEnvVars(rewritten)`) is an unconditional whole-list replace, followed by propagateChangedToEnvs(EnvVars:true) which rolls pods; unlike PatchService (RecordRevision at ~2355) the SetEnv path records no revision snapshot and audit logs names only, so wiped values are unrecoverable via kuso. The inconsistency and failure path are real. Severity corrected to P2, not P1: the destructive semantics are explicitly disclosed twice at call time (tool description AND the envVars jsonschema both say omitted keys are removed), the blast radius excludes secrets (set_secret/Secret untouched), it requires calling-agent error rather than any attacker action, and the server already offers a single-key delta endpoint (PUT .../env-vars/{name}) the MCP could expose — making this a missing-guardrail/consistency gap rather than a serious correctness defect.

title: Pinned/pre-manifest update paths deploy images with NO signature verification
summary: When a GitHub release has no release.json asset, both fetchVersion (pinned) and fetchLatest synthesize a manifest with hardcoded ghcr image tags and return before ever calling verifyManifestSignature, so the ed25519 supply-chain gate is silently skipped.
evidence: fetchVersion: `if manifestURL == "" { return &Manifest{ ... Components: {Server:{Image: ghcr.io/sislelabs/kuso-server-go:<tag>}, Operator:...}, CRDs:{URL: ...} }, nil }` (updater.go:395-411) returns before the signature block at line 439. fetchLatest does the same at updater.go:282-293 (`saveETag(); return rel.TagName, &Manifest{Version, Notes}, nil`) before the signature block at line 318. The embedded releasekey.pub is non-empty (44B ed25519 key) and requireSignatures() defaults true, so releases WITH a release.json are correctly gated — but omitting the asset defeats it entirely. The in-code comment at updater.go:425-430 explicitly states pinned upgrades must not bypass signing 'because that's exactly the path an attacker would prefer', yet this path does exactly that.
failure_scenario: Attacker who has hijacked the GitHub releases endpoint (the exact threat the embedded key at updater.go:940-954 claims to defend against) publishes a tag with a malicious ghcr.io/sislelabs/kuso-server-go:<tag> image and NO release.json asset. An admin (or automation) POSTs /api/system/update with {"version":"<tag>"} -> StartUpdate -> fetchVersion returns the synthesized, unverified manifest -> the updater Job runs `kubectl set image` to the attacker image on the server and operator deployments. Signature verification never runs. RCE on every self-updating cluster, with the supply-chain defense bypassed.
fix_sketch: Move the ErrUnsignedNoKey / verifyManifestSignature gate BEFORE the synth-manifest early returns (or run it on the synthesized path too): when requireSignatures() is true, a release with no release.json + no .sig must hard-fail rather than synthesize and proceed. At minimum, refuse to build a synth manifest for the pinned path when a public key is configured.
verify_reason: The code path is exactly as described. In fetchVersion (server-go/internal/updater/updater.go:395-411), when the GH release has no release.json asset, the function synthesizes a Manifest with hardcoded ghcr image tags (Server: ghcr.io/sislelabs/kuso-server-go:<tag>, Operator: kuso-operator:<tag>, CRDs URL) and `return`s at line 400-410 — BEFORE the verifyManifestSignature call at line 439. fetchLatest does the same at line 282-293 (saveETag + return with only Version/Notes) before its signature block at line 318. StartUpdate (line 640) with a pinned targetVersion calls fetchVersion (line 658), takes the returned manifest verbatim, and feeds m.Components.Server.Image / Operator.Image straight into the update Job env (lines 713-714), which runs `kubectl set image`. No downstream re-verification exists between the synthesized manifest and Job creation. The signature gate is therefore genuinely skipped on the no-manifest path. This directly contradicts the code's own comment at lines 425-430 ("Pinned upgrades shouldn't bypass signing because that's exactly the path an attacker would prefer"), and the embedded-key rationale at 940-954 that claims to defend against a hijacked GH/ghcr endpoint — a single GitHub-org compromise satisfies both the GH-releases-hijack and ghcr-push prerequisites, and the ed25519 gate that would otherwise stop it is never invoked. Note the pinned path (fetchVersion) is the real RCE vector because it hardcodes real ghcr image refs; the fetchLatest synth path leaves Components empty (no defensive fallback like fetchVersion's lines 472-477), so it yields a broken/empty-image Job rather than attacker code — the reviewer correctly centers the claim on fetchVersion.\n\nSeverity corrected P0 -> P1: this is a real, confirmed bypass of the intended supply-chain defense, but it is not directly exploitable against the system as-shipped. Weaponization requires (a) a prerequisite compromise of the upstream GitHub releases endpoint AND the ability to push a malicious image to ghcr.io/sislelabs (the synth path never trusts an attacker-supplied registry — it always pulls from the real ghcr repo), plus (b) an authenticated admin/automation invoking the pinned /api/system/update. It is a defense-in-depth mitigation silently defeated on a live path (serious security correctness bug with a plausible real trigger), which is P1 territory rather than a P0 that any attacker can trigger unaided. Pure api.github.com/DNS hijack without ghcr control degrades to a downgrade/rollback-forcing attack (force onto an old legitimate tag), still a real harm but bounded.

title: SMTP notification channel bypasses the SSRF-safe transport
summary: sendEmailSync -> smtpSendMail dials the operator-supplied SMTP host with a raw net.Dialer, never running the IsReservedIP check that the httpx SSRF-safe transport applies to every other notification channel.
evidence: smtpSendMail: `conn, err := dialer.DialContext(ctx, "tcp", net.JoinHostPort(host, port))` (channels.go:282) using a plain `&net.Dialer{...}` (channels.go:281). All HTTP channels (discord/webhook/slack/telegram/pushover) route through d.client whose Transport is httpx.SSRFSafeTransport() (notify.go:268), which rejects loopback/link-local/RFC1918. The email path has no such guard, and httpx/ssrf.go's package doc claims the transport is the guard for user-supplied outbound targets.
failure_scenario: An admin (notifications are AdminOnly, so single-tenant this is defense-in-depth, not privilege escalation) sets an email channel host to an internal address (e.g. 10.96.0.1 or 169.254.169.254); connection/timeout behavior differences let the kuso server be used to probe internal TCP ports the SSRF guard was meant to block. Because it silently diverges from the documented SSRF policy, a future move of email config to a less-privileged role would open a real SSRF hole.
fix_sketch: Resolve host and run httpx.IsReservedIP on the resolved IPs before dialing in smtpSendMail (or dial through a shared reserved-IP-checking dialer), matching the policy the HTTP channels already enforce.
verify_reason:
The claim is accurate on every code fact. channels.go:281-282 dials the operator-supplied SMTP host with a plain `&net.Dialer{Timeout: 15s}` via `dialer.DialContext(ctx, "tcp", net.JoinHostPort(host, port))` — no IsReservedIP check anywhere in smtpSendMail or sendEmailSync. By contrast, every HTTP channel (discord/webhook/slack/mattermost) posts through d.client whose Transport is httpx.SSRFSafeTransport() (notify.go:277), which resolves the host and rejects loopback/link-local/RFC1918/metadata IPs via IsReservedIP (ssrf.go:37-73, 85-107). telegram/pushover post to fixed vendor hosts and need no check. The email path is genuinely the only user-configurable outbound target that skips the guard.

I checked for validation elsewhere that could refute it: at config-set time validateNotificationConfig (notifications.go:455-458) for "email" only asserts host/from/to are non-empty — it does NOT call validateWebhookURL or any IP/hostname reserved-range check, whereas the URL channels do call validateWebhookURL (notifications.go:444) which blocks localhost, .svc/.cluster.local/.internal suffixes, and reserved IP literals. So the SMTP host is unguarded at BOTH validation and dispatch. The handler comment at ssrf.go and notifications.go even documents the SSRF transport as the intended guard for user-supplied outbound targets, and notify.go's neighbor code applies it — so email silently diverges from the stated policy. The DialContext/timeout behavior does give a distinguishable connect-vs-timeout signal usable to probe internal TCP ports (e.g. 10.96.0.1 kube apiserver, 169.254.169.254 IMDS on the SMTP port), which is exactly what SSRFSafeTransport exists to block.

Severity: the reviewer correctly frames this as defense-in-depth, not privilege escalation — notifications are admin-gated and kuso is single-tenant, so the actor already holds broad power and this is a narrow port-probe primitive (SMTP dials the metadata endpoint but doesn't speak HTTP to it, so IMDS token exfil isn't directly achievable; the practical yield is internal TCP port existence via connect/timeout timing). That is a real but narrow, admin-only, no-data-exfil bug. P2.
